entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Determination of the identity of chemical species in a sample (what is present), not the amount.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Determination of the amount or concentration of a chemical species in a sample.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A reproducible error from a flaw in the method, instrument, or operator that biases results consistently high or low. Can be detected and corrected.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Unpredictable scatter in repeated measurements from uncontrollable variables. Affects precision, not accuracy, and follows a Gaussian distribution.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A large, one-off mistake such as a spill or mis-reading that usually leads to discarding the datum.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Closeness of a measured value to the true or accepted value.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Closeness of repeated measurements to one another; quantified by standard deviation or RSD.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The sum of measurements divided by the number of measurements.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A measure of the spread of data about the mean. Small s means high precision.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The square of the standard deviation; useful for propagation of error.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Standard deviation expressed as a fraction or percent of the mean; a measure of relative precision.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The bell-shaped probability distribution that random errors follow. Characterized by mean μ and standard deviation σ.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A range about the mean within which the true population mean is expected to lie at a given confidence level.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Statistical test used to compare means (measured vs. known, or two measured means) to decide whether they differ significantly.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Test that compares two standard deviations (variances) to decide whether precision differs significantly between two data sets.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Statistical criterion for rejecting a suspected outlier in a small data set.
entries.key_detail: Q_exp = |gap| / range; reject if Q_exp > Q_table

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: An alternative outlier-rejection test that compares the outlier's deviation from the mean to the standard deviation.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Rules for combining individual measurement uncertainties into the total uncertainty of a calculated result.
entries.key_detail: Addition/subtraction: e = √(e₁² + e₂²); Multiplication/division: combine relative uncertainties.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Regression procedure that finds the best-fit straight line by minimizing the sum of squared residuals.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Plot of (y_measured − y_fit) vs x, used to assess whether a linear fit is appropriate.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Plot of instrument response vs. known analyte concentration used to determine unknown concentrations.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Lowest analyte concentration that produces a signal distinguishable from the blank with reasonable certainty.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Lowest concentration that can be determined with acceptable quantitative precision.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Ratio of analyte signal to the baseline noise; higher S/N means easier detection.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: y_dl = y_blank + 3·s_blank; the smallest signal statistically distinguishable from blank.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The overall system of policies and procedures that ensures data quality: use objectives, specifications, assessments.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A written statement of how analytical results will be used — drives method requirements.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Measurable criteria a method must meet (accuracy, precision, LOD, selectivity).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: False positive: reporting analyte present when it is not. False negative: reporting absent when it is present.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A sample containing all reagents and subjected to the full procedure but without analyte, to detect contamination and baseline response.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A blank prepared from reagents only, without going through the full sample prep steps.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A blank carried to and from the sampling site alongside real samples to detect contamination during transport.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A known amount of analyte added to a sample; percent recovered measures method accuracy in the matrix.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Periodic analysis of a standard to verify the calibration is still valid.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A blind sample of known value used to evaluate analyst / lab performance.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A formal, written, step-by-step procedure for carrying out a routine analysis.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A running chart of a measured value vs. time with warning and action limits, used to monitor process stability.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The documented process of proving that a method is fit for its intended use (linearity, accuracy, precision, LOD, LOQ, selectivity, robustness).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Quantitative performance parameters of a method: accuracy, precision, sensitivity, LOD, LOQ, dynamic range, selectivity.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The ability of a method to produce signal directly proportional to analyte concentration over a range.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The concentration range over which the method gives a useful response, from LOQ up to loss of linearity.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Precision obtained by the same analyst on the same instrument in a short time (intra-day).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Precision obtained across different labs, analysts, or instruments (inter-lab).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Measured amount of analyte divided by the true spiked amount, ×100; used to demonstrate accuracy.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Calibration by adding known increments of analyte directly to the sample to overcome matrix effects.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: A known amount of a different but similar compound added to all samples; ratio of analyte/IS signals corrects for sample losses and injection variability.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Alteration of analyte signal due to other sample components; corrected with standard addition or matrix-matched standards.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The large bulk sample taken from a lot to represent it.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The total material from which the gross sample is taken.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The smaller, homogenized portion of the gross sample delivered to the lab for analysis.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Contribution to the total uncertainty that arises from sample heterogeneity and sampling procedure, separate from analytical uncertainty.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Number of independent analyses required to achieve a desired confidence in the mean; grows as (t·s/E)².

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Logarithmic measure of light absorbed by a sample; dimensionless.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Fraction of incident light that passes through a sample.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Relates absorbance to concentration, path length, and molar absorptivity.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Wavelength-dependent proportionality constant in Beer's law; units M⁻¹ cm⁻¹.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Distance light travels through the absorbing sample; typically 1.00 cm cuvette.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Absorption spectroscopy in ultraviolet (190–400 nm) and visible (400–800 nm) regions; probes electronic transitions.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Quantitative technique where atoms in a flame or furnace absorb element-specific wavelengths from a hollow cathode lamp.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Measures light emitted by thermally or electrically excited atoms; e.g., ICP-OES.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Emission of light from a singlet excited state following absorption; occurs on ns timescale.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Emission from a triplet excited state via intersystem crossing; longer-lived than fluorescence.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Emission of light from products of a chemical reaction without external light excitation.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The wavelength difference between absorption and fluorescence emission maxima.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Functional group responsible for absorbing light at a particular wavelength.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Line source in AAS emitting sharp lines of a specific element from a cathode made of that element.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: AAS variant using a heated graphite tube for atomization; lower LOD than flame AA.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: High-temperature (~8000 K) argon plasma used to atomize/ionize samples for OES or MS.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Overlap of analyte emission/absorption with that of another species, biasing the measurement.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The fluid (liquid or gas) that carries the analyte through the stationary phase.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: The fixed material (column packing or coating) that interacts with analytes to separate them.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Plot of detector signal vs. time (or volume) showing peaks for each separated component.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Time between injection and elution of an analyte peak maximum.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Time for an unretained species to travel through the column.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Measure of how long an analyte is retained relative to mobile-phase time.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Ratio of retention factors of two peaks; measures ability to distinguish them.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Quantitative separation of two adjacent peaks; R_s ≥ 1.5 is baseline resolution.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Measure of column efficiency; higher N means narrower peaks.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Length of column equivalent to one theoretical plate; H = L/N. Smaller H = more efficient.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Relates plate height to mobile-phase linear velocity to find the optimum flow rate.
entries.key_detail: H = A + B/u + C·u (eddy, longitudinal, mass-transfer)
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Chromatography with a gaseous mobile phase for volatile analytes; separation by boiling point and interaction with stationary phase.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: High-Performance Liquid Chromatography; pressurized liquid mobile phase through small-particle packed columns.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Polar stationary phase (silica) with non-polar mobile phase; retains polar analytes longer.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Non-polar stationary phase (e.g., C18) with polar mobile phase; retains non-polar analytes longer.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Separation based on electrostatic interaction of ionic analytes with charged stationary-phase sites.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Separation by molecular size; large molecules elute first, excluded from pores.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Chromatographic separation at constant mobile-phase composition.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Systematic change of mobile-phase composition during the run to improve separation and speed.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Asymmetric peak with a trailing edge, often due to active sites on the stationary phase.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Common GC detector that ionizes combustible analytes in a hydrogen/air flame; measures current.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Non-destructive GC detector that senses differences in thermal conductivity between carrier gas and analyte.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Species that gains electrons (is reduced) in a redox reaction, causing oxidation of another species.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Species that loses electrons (is oxidized) in a redox reaction, causing reduction of another species.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Either the oxidation or the reduction portion of a redox reaction written separately with electrons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Reduction potential of a half-cell at standard conditions (1 M, 1 atm, 25 °C) vs. SHE.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Reduction potential measured in a specified medium (given ionic strength, pH) rather than at thermodynamic standard state.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Formal potential evaluated at pH = 7; used for biological redox couples.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Measured voltage of a complete electrochemical cell under non-standard conditions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Gives electrode or cell potential as a function of concentrations at non-standard conditions.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.term: Gibbs Free Energy and Cell Potential
entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Relates spontaneity of a redox reaction to its cell voltage.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: At equilibrium, ΔG = 0 and E_cell = 0, giving log K = n·E°/0.05916 at 25 °C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Electrode whose potential depends on the activity of the analyte being measured.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Electrode with a stable, known, reproducible potential independent of the sample (e.g., Ag/AgCl, SCE).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Primary reference electrode, Pt|H₂(1 atm)|H⁺(1 M), assigned E° = 0 V.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Reference electrode based on Hg/Hg₂Cl₂ in saturated KCl; E = +0.241 V vs. SHE.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Common reference electrode, Ag|AgCl|Cl⁻; potential depends on [Cl⁻].
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Electrode at which the electrochemical reaction of interest takes place in a 3-electrode cell.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Electrode that completes the circuit and carries current in a 3-electrode cell without affecting the working electrode.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Small potential at the interface between two electrolyte solutions of different composition due to different ion mobilities.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Indicator electrode made of a metal in contact with its ion; potential set by the metal/ion couple.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Indicator electrode with a membrane that develops a potential depending selectively on the activity of one ion.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Quantifies an ISE's response to interfering ions relative to the target ion; smaller is better.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Most common pH ISE; thin glass membrane responds to H⁺ activity via ion exchange.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: At high pH, glass electrode responds partially to Na⁺, indicating pH lower than actual.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: At very low pH (<0), glass electrode indicates pH higher than actual due to membrane saturation.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Measurement of analyte activity via the potential of an indicator electrode vs. a reference electrode at zero current.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Titration monitored by following the potential of an indicator electrode.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Driving a non-spontaneous redox reaction by applying an external voltage; mass deposited follows Faraday's law.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Extra voltage beyond thermodynamic requirement needed to drive an electrochemical reaction at a useful rate.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Voltage loss due to solution resistance; equals i·R.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Shift in electrode potential from depletion/buildup of reactant at the electrode surface because mass transport lags reaction rate.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Electroanalytical technique measuring current at a fixed applied potential; current is proportional to analyte concentration.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Technique measuring current while the applied potential is varied; yields i-E curves.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Voltammetry that sweeps potential forward then reverse, giving a duck-shaped i–E curve with anodic/cathodic peaks.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Difference between anodic and cathodic peak potentials in CV; ~59/n mV for a reversible n-electron couple.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Electron transfer fast enough that Nernstian equilibrium holds at the electrode; ΔEp ≈ 59/n mV.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Electron transfer so slow that one sweep direction shows no peak.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Intermediate kinetics; ΔEp larger than 59/n mV and scan-rate-dependent.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Device coupling a biological recognition element (enzyme, antibody) to a transducer for analyte detection.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Amperometric biosensor using glucose oxidase to convert glucose to H₂O₂ or mediator, producing a current proportional to [glucose].

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Q = I·t; total electrons passed, in coulombs.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Rate of flow of charge, in amperes (C/s).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Electrical work w = −n·F·E; energy transferred by cell to surroundings.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: P = I·V; rate of energy transfer, in watts.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: R = V/I (Ohm's law); opposition to current flow, in ohms.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Charge of one mole of electrons, 96 485 C/mol.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Ratio of product to reactant activities at equilibrium; characterizes extent of reaction.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Thermodynamic potential; ΔG < 0 means spontaneous. Related to K by ΔG° = −RT ln K.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: When a system at equilibrium is stressed, it shifts to counteract the stress.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Equilibrium constant for dissolution of a slightly soluble ionic solid; product of ion activities.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Decrease in solubility of an ionic solid caused by adding a soluble salt sharing one of its ions (Le Châtelier).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Product-to-reactant ratio with current (non-equilibrium) concentrations; compare to Ksp to predict precipitation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Sequential precipitation of ions by exploiting large differences in Ksp values.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Acid or base that dissociates completely in water (HCl, NaOH).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Partial dissociation; characterized by Ka or Kb < 1.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Donates or accepts a single proton (e.g., HCl, NH₃).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Two ionizable protons; two dissociation steps with Ka1, Ka2 (e.g., H₂CO₃).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Three ionizable protons; three Ka values (e.g., H₃PO₄).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Equilibrium constant for HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻; pKa = −log Ka.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Equilibrium constant for B + H₂O ⇌ BH⁺ + OH⁻.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: For a conjugate acid/base pair, product equals Kw = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25 °C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: −log a(H⁺); measures hydrogen ion activity.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Hydrated metal cations (e.g., Fe(H₂O)₆³⁺) donate protons from coordinated water, lowering pH.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Sum of each ion's molarity times the square of its charge, divided by 2.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Effective thermodynamic concentration; a = γ·c, used in place of concentration in rigorous equilibria.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Correction factor relating activity to concentration; approaches 1 at low ionic strength.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Predicts activity coefficient as a function of ionic strength and ion size.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Debye–Hückel form valid up to μ ≈ 0.1 M, including the ion-size parameter α.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Statement that total moles of each element are conserved across all species.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Total positive charge = total negative charge in a solution (electroneutrality).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: General approach using mass balance, charge balance, and equilibrium expressions to solve complex systems.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Dominant acid/base form of a polyprotic system at a given pH, found by comparing pH to pKa values.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Fraction of total analyte present as a given species at a given pH.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Solution of a weak acid and its conjugate base that resists pH change on addition of small amounts of acid or base.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Relates buffer pH to pKa and the ratio of conjugate base to acid.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Moles of strong acid/base required to change buffer pH by 1 unit; maximum near pH = pKa.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Titration point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte (stoichiometric).
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Experimentally observed point where the indicator signals completion of titration; slightly differs from equivalence point.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Difference between end point and equivalence point volumes.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Titration based on an oxidation-reduction reaction; progress followed potentiometrically or with redox indicator.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Species that changes color over a small potential range (E° ± 0.05916/n) to signal the equivalence point.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Titration where analyte precipitates with titrant (e.g., Ag⁺ + Cl⁻ → AgCl); followed potentiometrically.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Optical device that selects a narrow band of wavelengths from a polychromatic source, usually via a grating or prism.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Optical element with closely spaced grooves that disperses light into its constituent wavelengths.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Aperture controlling the spectral bandwidth reaching the detector; narrower slit → higher resolution, lower throughput.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Highly sensitive vacuum-tube detector that amplifies a photoelectron cascade through successive dynodes.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Semiconductor detector that produces a current proportional to incident light intensity.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Array of photodiodes that captures a full spectrum simultaneously; common in UV-Vis and HPLC.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: 2D array detector with high sensitivity and low noise; used in Raman and fluorescence spectrometers.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Device that supplies electromagnetic radiation (e.g., deuterium lamp for UV, tungsten for visible, hollow cathode for AA).
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Optically transparent sample cell, usually 1.00 cm path length, used in absorbance measurements.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Internal electrode of a PMT that amplifies electrons via secondary emission.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Spectrometer design where light is split between sample and reference paths for real-time background correction.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Chemistry — Analytical
entries.definition: Device that converts a liquid sample into a fine aerosol for introduction into a flame or plasma.
entries.sources: standard reference (no source file had text)

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Instantaneous rate of change of a function; slope of the tangent line, f′(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x+h)−f(x)]/h.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Derivative of a constant is zero.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Derivative of xʳ is r·xʳ⁻¹ for any real r.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Derivative of eˣ is itself.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Derivative of aˣ picks up a factor of ln a.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Derivative of ln x is 1/x for x > 0.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Derivative of log_a x is 1/(x ln a).

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[sin x] = cos x.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[cos x] = −sin x.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[tan x] = sec² x.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[cot x] = −csc² x.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[sec x] = sec x · tan x.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[csc x] = −csc x · cot x.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[sin⁻¹ x] = 1/√(1 − x²).

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[cos⁻¹ x] = −1/√(1 − x²).

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[tan⁻¹ x] = 1/(1 + x²).

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[cot⁻¹ x] = −1/(1 + x²).

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Derivative of a product of two functions.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Derivative of a quotient of two functions.

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: Derivative of a composite function f(g(x)).

entries.subject: Math — Calculus
entries.definition: d/dx[g(x)ʳ] = r·g(x)ʳ⁻¹·g′(x).

entries.subject: Math — Calculus

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms, bounded by a plasma membrane and containing DNA and ribosomes.
entries.key_detail: Cells come from other cells (Omnis cellula e cellula).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The foundational principle stating that all organisms are made of cells, the cell is the smallest living unit, and cells come from other cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientist who published Micrographia (1665) and first named the units he observed in cork "cells".

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: First person to observe live cells using an early microscope in the 1670s.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A cell type in which DNA is NOT contained within a membrane-bound nucleus; includes bacteria and archaea; generally small.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A cell type in which DNA is contained within a nucleus; includes protists, fungi, plants, and animals; generally large and contains membrane-bound organelles.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The lipid bilayer that separates the cell interior from the external environment, present in all cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The cellular machine for protein synthesis, present in all cells, either free in the cytosol or bound to the rough ER.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The membrane-bound organelle that stores DNA in eukaryotic cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Eukaryotic organelle consisting of interconnected membranous sacs and tubules; site of protein and lipid synthesis.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Stack of flat sacs (cisternae) that processes and sorts proteins received from the ER.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Membrane-bound sac that moves materials between organelles and between the cell and its environment.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Acidic, membrane-bound organelle that degrades macromolecules using hydrolytic enzymes.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Network of protein filaments inside the cell that provides shape, organization, and enables movement.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Double-membrane-bound organelle that carries out cellular respiration to produce ATP.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Plant-cell organelle that performs photosynthesis.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Organelle that breaks down long-chain fatty acids.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A membrane-bound subcellular compartment separated from the cytosol that contains function-specific molecules and proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Technique used to separate cellular components (organelles) for biochemical study, central to modern cell biology.
entries.key_detail: Developed by Porter, Palade, Claude, de Duve in the 1940s-1950s.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: High-resolution imaging technique used to visualize internal cell structures beyond the resolution of light microscopy.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Lipid-based membrane that separates the cell (or organelle) interior from its surroundings.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Describes molecules that contain both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions; characteristic of membrane lipids.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A glycerol-based phospholipid with two fatty-acid tails and a phosphate-linked head group; a primary class of membrane lipid.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A class of membrane lipid built on a sphingosine backbone, enriched in lipid rafts.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A class of lipid (e.g., cholesterol) containing a fused ring structure, embedded in membranes to regulate fluidity.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Lipid molecule with one or more sugars covalently attached; located only on the non-cytosolic side of the bilayer.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Two-layer sheet of lipid molecules with polar heads facing outward and hydrophobic tails facing inward, forming the core of biomembranes.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An artificial lipid bilayer vesicle used for membrane research and drug delivery.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A planar artificial lipid bilayer formed across an aperture, used to study membrane properties.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The ability of lipids and proteins to move laterally within the membrane; necessary for membrane function.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The change of a membrane between a gel-like state and a fluid-like state at a characteristic temperature (freezing point).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The temperature at which a membrane transitions between gel and fluid states.
entries.key_detail: Unsaturated/short tails decrease it; saturated/long tails increase it.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A fatty-acid tail with no double bonds; packs tightly and raises membrane freezing point.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A fatty-acid tail containing one or more double bonds that kink the chain; lowers membrane freezing point and increases fluidity.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Fluorescence Recovery After Photobleaching; technique that measures lateral diffusion by bleaching a small region and monitoring recovery of fluorescence from neighboring molecules.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Membrane microdomain rich in sphingolipids and cholesterol that does not mix easily with other regions and may organize membrane proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A sterol lipid that intercalates between phospholipids to modulate membrane fluidity and is enriched in lipid rafts.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Differential distribution of lipid (and protein) species between the two leaflets of the bilayer; essential for function.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The outer leaflet of the plasma membrane bilayer, enriched in phosphatidylcholine (PC), sphingomyelin (SM), and glycolipids.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The inner leaflet of the plasma membrane bilayer, enriched in phosphatidylserine (PS) and phosphatidylethanolamine (PE).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A major phosphoglyceride preferentially found in the exoplasmic leaflet.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A sphingolipid preferentially found in the exoplasmic leaflet and enriched in lipid rafts.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A phosphoglyceride restricted to the cytosolic leaflet; its exposure on the outer leaflet is an apoptotic signal.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A phosphoglyceride preferentially found in the cytosolic leaflet of the plasma membrane.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A minor phospholipid found only in the cytosolic monolayer that functions in cytosolic signaling.
entries.key_detail: Would be useless in the outer monolayer.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A protein that does the "work" of membranes, including receptor, transport, and enzymatic functions; classified as integral or peripheral.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A protein embedded in the hydrophobic core of the membrane, attached via alpha-helices, beta-barrels, or non-protein anchors.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A protein attached to the membrane surface through ionic bonds with integral proteins or lipids; removable by high salt.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An integral membrane protein whose polypeptide crosses the bilayer exactly once via a single alpha-helix.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An integral membrane protein that crosses the bilayer multiple times using multiple start- and stop-transfer sequences.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Integral membrane protein attached via an alpha-helix embedded in only one leaflet of the bilayer.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Integral membrane protein whose transmembrane region consists of a cylindrical sheet of beta-strands.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A hydrophobic molecule (e.g., lipid) covalently attached to a protein that anchors it to the membrane (e.g., GPI anchor).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A hydrophobic-amino-acid-rich stretch of roughly 20 residues sufficient for an alpha-helix to span the hydrophobic core.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A graph of average hydrophobicity along a protein sequence used to predict membrane-spanning alpha-helices.
entries.key_detail: Calculated in a sliding window (e.g., residues 1-20, 2-21, etc.).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A protein with covalently attached oligosaccharides that function in cell identity; sugars appear only on the non-cytosolic side.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Technique using high salt (e.g., 0.6 M NaCl) to release peripheral proteins from vesicles while leaving integral proteins behind.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An isolated membrane vesicle whose original outer surface remains on the outside.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A vesicle generated by freeze-thaw cycling in which the original cytosolic surface now faces outward, allowing access to internal peripheral proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Technique using a protease cocktail to degrade only accessible external protein domains; used to map integral protein topology.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Amphipathic molecule used to dissolve hydrophobic membrane regions in aqueous solution and purify integral membrane proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Sodium dodecyl sulfate; a strong detergent used with beta-mercaptoethanol (bME) to dissolve membrane lipids and proteins for gel separation by size.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The current model of the plasma membrane in which a fluid lipid bilayer contains a mosaic of mobile proteins and lipids.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Classical cell-fusion experiment demonstrating that membrane proteins mix between fused cells, proving membrane fluidity.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The observation that not all membrane proteins diffuse freely; many are confined by aggregates, extracellular/intracellular interactions, neighbor-cell contacts, or cytoskeletal fences.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A plasma-membrane region where the underlying cytoskeleton restricts lateral movement of proteins; proteins can escape but rarely do.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The ability of a membrane to allow different small molecules to cross at different rates depending on size and charge.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An integral membrane protein that enables transport of ions and polar molecules across the bilayer; three classes are channels, pumps, and transporters.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A transport protein that forms a pore for passive diffusion of ions or molecules with their gradient; often gated.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Passive transport of molecules across a membrane through a channel or uniporter, moving down their gradient without energy input.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A channel that can open and close in response to stimuli (voltage, ligand, mechanical).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The property by which a channel allows only certain ions/molecules to pass, determined by pore size and chemical interactions.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A transport protein that uses ATP energy to move ions or molecules against their gradient (active transport).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Transport of a solute against its electrochemical gradient that requires energy input.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Transport of solutes down their gradient without an energy input; includes simple and facilitated diffusion.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Active transport in which ATP hydrolysis directly drives solute movement (e.g., Na+/K+ ATPase).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Coupled transport in which the gradient of one solute (built by primary active transport) provides energy to move another solute against its gradient.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A primary active-transport pump that moves 3 Na+ out of the cell and 2 K+ in per ATP hydrolyzed, establishing the Na+ and K+ gradients.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientist who discovered the Na+/K+ pump; 1997 Nobel Prize.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A membrane protein that shuttles solutes by alternating between two conformations; slower than channels.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A transporter that moves a single type of molecule across the membrane by conformation change; passive transport.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A coupled transporter that moves two solutes across the membrane in the same direction.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A coupled transporter that moves two solutes in opposite directions.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Transport that uses the gradient of one solute as energy to move another; achieved by conformational changes (symporter or antiporter).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A symporter that uses the Na+ gradient to transport glucose against its gradient; a classic example of secondary active transport.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The combined concentration and electrical gradient of an ion across a membrane that drives transport.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The process by which newly synthesized proteins are directed to their correct organelle or subcellular location.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Mechanism in which translation of a protein and its translocation into the ER lumen occur at the same time.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Protein import into an organelle after translation is complete (compare with co-translational).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A 15-30 amino-acid N-terminal stretch rich in hydrophobic residues (at least 6 consecutive) that targets proteins to the ER.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A cytosolic ribonucleoprotein that binds the signal sequence as it emerges from the ribosome and pauses translation.
entries.key_detail: Hydrolyzes GTP to release from the SR.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An integral ER membrane protein that binds SRP to deliver the ribosome-nascent chain complex to the translocon; hydrolyzes GTP.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The protein-conducting channel in the ER membrane through which nascent polypeptides are threaded during co-translational translocation.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: ER membrane protease that cleaves (removes) the signal sequence from a translocating polypeptide, releasing the mature protein into the ER lumen.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The longer, immature form of a secreted protein that still contains its N-terminal signal sequence; example used was 25 kD preprolactin vs. 22 kD prolactin.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientist who used reconstitution experiments (1970s-80s) to work out the co-translational translocation mechanism; 1999 Nobel Prize.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Membrane-bound fragment of smooth or rough ER formed during cell disruption; functionally active for studying ER processes.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Microsomes derived from the rough ER used in reconstitution assays; contain translocon, SRP receptor, and signal peptidase.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Rough microsomes washed with high salt to remove peripheral proteins, notably SRP; cannot support translocation alone.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Salt-stripped RM additionally treated with elastase to remove the cytosolic domain of SR; cannot relieve the SRP pause.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The cytosolic portion of the SRP receptor; sufficient to relieve the SRP-induced translational pause.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An in vitro translation extract that contains SRP.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An in vitro translation extract that lacks SRP; used to study SRP function by add-back.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An internal hydrophobic signal that initiates threading of a polypeptide through the translocon; seen in single- and multi-pass proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An internal hydrophobic sequence that halts translocation and anchors the polypeptide in the membrane.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The orientation of a transmembrane protein's domains relative to the ER/plasma-membrane bilayer.
entries.key_detail: Orientation set by charges around the signal sequence (+ on cytosolic side).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Region of the ER with ribosomes attached to the cytosolic side of the membrane; site of synthesis of secreted and membrane proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Region of the ER lacking ribosomes; primary site of lipid synthesis.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The pathway by which proteins move from the rough ER through the Golgi into vesicles and then to the cell exterior.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Technique in which cells are briefly exposed to a radiolabeled amino acid ("pulse") and then excess unlabeled amino acid ("chase") to track the movement of labeled proteins over time.
entries.key_detail: Defined the secretory pathway in pancreatic acinar cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientist who used pulse-chase and electron microscopy to define the secretory pathway in pancreatic acinar cells (late 1960s).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Exocrine pancreatic cell used by Palade as a model for secretion; high rate of digestive-enzyme secretion and distinct morphology.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Immature secretory vesicle emerging from the trans-Golgi network, seen in pulse-chase at ~37 min.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Covalent attachment of a preassembled 14-sugar oligosaccharide onto asparagine residues of proteins in the ER lumen.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The ER enzyme that transfers the preassembled sugar chain from dolichol onto an asparagine of a target protein.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Lipid carrier in the ER membrane on which the N-linked oligosaccharide precursor is assembled prior to transfer.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Protein in the ER lumen (e.g., calnexin, BiP) that assists folding of newly translocated proteins; folding status is linked to glycosylation state.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: An ER membrane chaperone that binds monoglucosylated N-glycans to assist folding of glycoproteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Major ER-lumen HSP70 chaperone involved in protein folding and in regulating the unfolded protein response (IRE1 pathway).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: ER stress-response pathway triggered by accumulation of unfolded proteins; one branch is the IRE1 pathway.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A branch of the UPR in which IRE1, normally held inactive by BiP, oligomerizes and initiates a response to unfolded proteins in the ER.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: ER-specific process of forming covalent S-S bonds between cysteine residues during protein folding.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Activation of a protein by cleavage of a precursor form; one of the ER's functions for some secreted proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A flat membrane sac; Golgi cisternae are stacked and contain distinct enzymes at each level.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The Golgi face nearest the ER; receives COPII vesicles from the ER and is where mannose-6-phosphate is added to lysosomal enzymes.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The middle cisternae of the Golgi stack, where intermediate glycosylation modifications occur.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The Golgi face farthest from the ER; site of late glycosylation and entry into the trans-Golgi network.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The sorting station at the trans face of the Golgi that dispatches vesicles to lysosomes, endosomes, or the plasma membrane.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The model in which secretory cargo stays in a single cisterna while Golgi enzymes move retrograde, so the cisterna itself "matures" from cis to trans.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A vesicle coated with COPII proteins that transports cargo from the ER to the Golgi.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A vesicle coated with COPI proteins that mediates retrograde transport within the Golgi and from the Golgi back to the ER.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: N-terminal sorting sequence on soluble ER cargo that binds the Surf4 receptor for packaging into COPII vesicles.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Transmembrane receptor in the ER that binds the FPF signal on soluble cargo for COPII packaging.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A di-acidic sorting signal in the cytosolic domain of transmembrane cargo that binds Sec24 of the COPII coat.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A C-terminal retrieval signal on soluble ER-resident proteins recognized (pH-dependently) by the KDEL receptor for return to the ER via COPI.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A C-terminal retrieval signal on ER-resident transmembrane proteins for return to the ER in COPI vesicles.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Sorting signal on transmembrane proteins that binds the AP1 complex of the clathrin coat for exocytosis from the TGN.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Continuous, unregulated release of soluble proteins from the cell via vesicles that fuse directly with the plasma membrane.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Release of soluble proteins stored in secretory vesicles that fuse with the plasma membrane only in response to a signal.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The process of fusing vesicles with the plasma membrane to release contents to the extracellular space.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Soluble degradative enzyme active at the acidic pH of the lysosome; responsible for macromolecule breakdown.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientist who discovered the lysosome; 1974 Nobel Prize.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A phosphate-modified mannose sugar on N-glycans of soluble lysosomal enzymes that serves as the sorting tag for delivery to lysosomes.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Transmembrane receptor in the TGN that binds M6P-tagged lysosomal enzymes for packaging into vesicles that fuse with the late endosome.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Acidic endocytic compartment that receives M6P-tagged vesicles from the TGN; low pH releases cargo and phosphate is removed before lysosome delivery.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A late-endosomal compartment containing internal vesicles formed by inward budding, allowing cytosolic domains of transmembrane proteins to be degraded in the lysosome.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Endosomal Sorting Complexes Required for Transport; protein machinery that drives inward budding at the late endosome to form multivesicular bodies.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Cellular self-digestion process in which cytoplasmic material is enclosed in an autophagosome that fuses with a lysosome for degradation.
entries.key_detail: Yoshinori Ohsumi received the 2016 Nobel Prize for this work.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientist who used random mutagenesis of yeast and complementation to characterize autophagy-related (Atg) genes; 2016 Nobel Prize.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Autophagy-related genes identified in yeast screens that encode components of the autophagy machinery.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Initiation site for autophagosome formation where components to be degraded are recruited via selective autophagy receptors.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Receptor that recognizes specific cargo and recruits it to the PAS for autophagic degradation.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Autophagy protein recruited to the PAS along with COPII vesicles during early autophagosome formation.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Autophagy protein required to add additional vesicles during autophagosome growth.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Double-membrane vesicle containing cytoplasmic cargo that ultimately fuses with the lysosome for degradation.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Movement of cargo between the ER, Golgi, lysosomes, and exterior in membrane-bound vesicles, obviating the need for cargo to cross membranes.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Coat protein forming a lattice on vesicles that bud between the Golgi and cell membrane and during receptor-mediated endocytosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Formation of a vesicle from a donor membrane, driven by coat-protein-induced curvature.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Merging of a vesicle with its target membrane, mediated by SNAREs pulling the bilayers into contact.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A small GTPase that regulates COPII coat formation; Sar1-GDP is cytosolic, Sar1-GTP is membrane-bound via an exposed hydrophobic tail.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: ER-membrane guanine nucleotide exchange factor (GEF) that activates Sar1 by promoting exchange of GDP for GTP.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Protein that activates small GTPases by promoting exchange of GDP for GTP.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Protein that stimulates hydrolysis of GTP to GDP on a small GTPase, switching it off.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Inner-coat COPII complex recruited by Sar1-GTP; curves the membrane and binds cargo receptors (Sec24 binds DXE).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Outer-coat COPII complex recruited by Sec23/24; forms the outer cage of COPII vesicles.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: GTPase that wraps around the neck of a budding clathrin-coated vesicle and uses GTP hydrolysis to pinch it off.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Disassembly of a coat after budding; for COPII, triggered by Sar1 GTP hydrolysis and release from the membrane.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Small GTPase family (>60 human members) that confers vesicle target specificity; Rab-GTP is lipid-anchored to membranes, Rab-GDP is cytosolic.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Protein recruited by Rab-GTP that helps tether an incoming vesicle to its target membrane before SNARE engagement.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Integral membrane protein family that mediates membrane fusion; dozens of types distributed among different membranes.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: SNARE protein present on the vesicle membrane that pairs with a t-SNARE on the target membrane during fusion.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: SNARE protein present on the target membrane that pairs with v-SNAREs to drive vesicle fusion.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: ATPase that, together with ATP, dissociates v-/t-SNARE complexes after fusion so v-SNAREs can be recycled to the donor membrane.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientist who identified yeast sec mutants defective in secretion; shared 2013 Nobel Prize.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientist who identified SNAREs and NSF as the machinery for vesicle fusion; shared 2013 Nobel Prize.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Uptake of external material into the cell in membrane-bound vesicles; includes phagocytosis, pinocytosis, and receptor-mediated endocytosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Endocytic ingestion of large particles such as whole bacteria by specialized cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Endocytic ingestion of fluids and dissolved solutes in small vesicles.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Selective uptake of ligands bound to cell-surface receptors via enrichment in clathrin-coated vesicles; example ligands include LDL and transferrin.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Lipoprotein particle containing cholesterol, phospholipids, and ApoB, used to deliver cholesterol to cells via the LDL receptor.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Protein component of LDL particles that is recognized by the LDL receptor at the cell surface.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Cell-surface receptor that binds ApoB on LDL particles at pH 7 and releases them at pH 5 in the late endosome via pH-dependent conformational change.
entries.key_detail: In LE, ligand-binding arm binds its own beta-propeller to release LDL.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Iron-carrying ligand that remains bound to its receptor during RME; Fe3+ is released at low pH in the late endosome.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientists who discovered the LDL receptor and characterized receptor-mediated endocytosis; 1985 Nobel Prize.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Cytoskeletal filament 7-9 nm in diameter composed of actin subunits.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Cytoskeletal filament ~10 nm in diameter with a tetrameric subunit; very strong, rope-like, and lacks polarity (so no motors).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Cytoskeletal filament 25 nm in diameter composed of alpha/beta-tubulin heterodimers arranged as 13 protofilaments forming a tube; polar.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A cytoskeletal assembly that rapidly polymerizes and depolymerizes (e.g., the mitotic spindle).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A cytoskeletal assembly that persists long-term (e.g., in intestinal epithelial cells).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Globular monomeric form of actin that binds ATP or ADP; the subunit of microfilaments.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Filamentous polymer of actin with polar (+) and (-) ends; the functional state in cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Formation of a trimer that initiates an actin filament; the rate-limiting step of polymerization.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Steady-state condition in which actin subunits add to the (+) end and dissociate from the (-) end of a filament.
entries.key_detail: ATP hydrolysis and Pi release occur within the filament.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Fast-growing end of an actin filament or microtubule.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Slow-growing end of an actin filament or microtubule; for microtubules, anchored at the MTOC.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Fungal toxin that binds the (+) end of actin filaments to prevent polymerization.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Fungal toxin (Angel of Death mushroom) that binds along the sides of actin filaments to stabilize them.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Whole-cell locomotion driven by coordinated actin polymerization and depolymerization.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Plasma-membrane complex that attaches the actin cytoskeleton to the extracellular matrix through integrins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Transmembrane receptor linking the actin cytoskeleton to the extracellular matrix at focal adhesions.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Contractile bundle of actin and myosin anchored at focal adhesions; formed under Rho activation.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Flat, sheet-like, actin-rich extension at the leading edge of a crawling cell; driven by Rac and the Arp2/3 complex.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Finger-like actin-bundled extension at the leading edge; formed under Cdc42 control.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Small GTPase that triggers stress fiber formation and contraction.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Small GTPase that drives lamellipodium formation, in part by activating WAVE and Arp2/3.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Small GTPase that drives filopodium formation by activating WASp and Arp2/3.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Seven-subunit nucleating complex whose Arp2 and Arp3 subunits mimic an actin nucleus; binds to the side of an existing filament to create a branch, remaining at the (-) end.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Nucleation-promoting factor activated by Cdc42-GTP; relieves intramolecular autoinhibition to expose the Arp2/3-activating domain.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Nucleation-promoting factor activated downstream of Rac to stimulate Arp2/3-dependent branched actin at lamellipodia.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Protein that caps the (+) end of an actin filament to prevent further growth.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Actin-severing protein that binds along ADP-actin regions of filaments and depolymerizes them from (-) ends.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Monomer-binding protein that increases nucleotide exchange on G-actin, making more ATP-actin available for polymerization.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Microtubule subunit protein; microtubules are built from alpha/beta-tubulin heterodimers.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: One of the two subunits of the tubulin heterodimer; binds GTP that is not hydrolyzed.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Tubulin subunit that binds and hydrolyzes GTP during microtubule growth; a GTP cap stabilizes the (+) end.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A linear strand of tubulin dimers; 13 protofilaments associate laterally to form a microtubule tube.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Intracellular site of microtubule nucleation that anchors (-) ends; types include centrosomes, spindle poles, and basal bodies.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Primary animal-cell MTOC containing a pair of centrioles surrounded by pericentriolar material rich in gamma-TuRCs.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Cylindrical structure of nine microtubule triplets; a pair makes up the centrosome.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Amorphous matrix around centrioles that contains gamma-TuRCs and other factors for microtubule nucleation.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Ring-shaped complex of gamma-tubulin that nucleates new microtubules at the MTOC.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A stabilizing region of GTP-bound beta-tubulin at the (+) end of a growing microtubule; its loss triggers catastrophe.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Stochastic switching of a microtubule's (+) end between growth and rapid shrinkage, governed by the GTP cap.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Microtubule toxin from meadow saffron that prevents polymerization.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Microtubule-stabilizing drug from Pacific yew bark; a common anti-cancer agent.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Family of microtubule-based motor proteins, most of which walk toward the (+) end using 8 nm steps powered by ATP hydrolysis.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Family of microtubule-based motor proteins that walk toward the (-) end, taking 8 nm (or multiples of 8 nm) steps powered by ATP.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Protein that uses ATP hydrolysis to generate directed movement along a cytoskeletal track.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Hair-like, microtubule-based extension of the cell surface; eukaryotic cilia and flagella share the same axonemal structure.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Microtubule-based core of a cilium or flagellum (without the surrounding membrane); has a characteristic 9+2 organization.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Cylindrical MTOC at the base of a cilium with 9 microtubule triplets; the A and B microtubules extend into the cilium.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Characteristic cross-section of a motile axoneme with 9 outer microtubule doublets surrounding a central pair of singlets.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Dynein motor in axonemes (outer and inner arms) that walks toward the (-) end of adjacent doublets to drive ciliary bending.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Scientist who isolated the first microtubule motor (outer arm dynein) from Tetrahymena cilia in 1963.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Elastic protein that tethers adjacent outer doublets in the axoneme, helping convert dynein-driven sliding into bending.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Axonemal structure projecting from outer doublets to the central sheath; involved in regulating ciliary beating.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Coordination in which only dyneins on one half of the ciliary cross-section are active at a given time, so sliding produces productive bending.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The process of cilium growth; components synthesized in the cell body are added to the distal tip.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Motor-driven transport system that moves ciliary cargo in "IFT trains" along axonemal microtubules.
entries.key_detail: Anterograde by kinesin 2; retrograde by dynein 2.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Anterograde IFT motor that moves cargo toward the ciliary tip.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Retrograde IFT motor that returns IFT cargo from the ciliary tip to the cell body.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: The basic subunit of an intermediate filament; these stack side-by-side to form a non-polar, rope-like filament.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Intermediate filament protein found primarily in epithelial cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Nuclear intermediate filament protein that forms the nuclear lamina underlying the inner nuclear membrane.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Intermediate filament type found in neuronal axons; contributes to axonal mechanical strength.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Double lipid-bilayer membrane around the nucleus; the outer membrane is continuous with the ER, while the inner interacts with chromatin and the nuclear lamina.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Large multiprotein complex spanning the nuclear envelope that regulates movement of molecules between nucleus and cytoplasm; components include a structural ring, cytoplasmic filaments, and nuclear basket.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: One of dozens of proteins making up the NPC; many contain FG-repeats in unstructured domains that form the diffusion barrier.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Phenylalanine-glycine repeats in unstructured nucleoporin domains that form the selective permeability barrier of the NPC and bind transport receptors.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A sorting sequence necessary and sufficient for nuclear import; first identified in SV40 T-antigen and functional when added anywhere in a cytosolic protein.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A typically leucine-rich sequence that marks a protein for nuclear export via exportins.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Receptor that binds NLS cargo and FG-repeats to ferry proteins from the cytoplasm into the nucleus.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Receptor that binds NES cargo together with Ran-GTP and FG-repeats to transport cargo out of the nucleus.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Small GTP-binding protein required for both nuclear import and export; Ran-GTP is nuclear while Ran-GDP is cytosolic.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: GTPase-activating protein for Ran, localized to the cytosol; keeps cytosolic Ran in the GDP-bound state.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Guanine nucleotide exchange factor for Ran, localized to the nucleus; keeps nuclear Ran in the GTP-bound state.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Importin-cargo complex diffuses through the NPC via FG-repeat interactions; in the nucleus, Ran-GTP binding to importin triggers cargo release.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Ran-GTP promotes binding of exportin to NES cargo in the nucleus; in the cytosol, GTP hydrolysis releases cargo and recycles the exportin.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Heterodimeric transport receptor that binds mature mRNA and FG-repeats to export mRNA from the nucleus.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Cytosolic helicase at the NPC that removes NXF1/NXT1 from mRNA to ensure directional export.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
