entries.definition: Transcription factor involved in the immune response that shuttles between nucleus and cytosol; its NLS is masked when phosphorylated and unmasked by activated calcineurin.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Ca2+-activated phosphatase that dephosphorylates NF-AT, unmasking its NLS and masking its NES to promote nuclear import.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Regulated movement of a protein between cytosol and nucleus, often through masking/unmasking of its NLS and NES.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Short incubation with a radiolabeled precursor (e.g., 3H-leucine) to mark newly synthesized molecules during a defined window.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Subsequent incubation with excess unlabeled precursor to follow the fate of previously labeled molecules over time.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Approach in which a process is disassembled into components that are recombined in various combinations to define the role of each; used by Blobel to dissect co-translational translocation.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: A non-hydrolyzable GTP analog used experimentally to trap GTPases in their active state.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Gel electrophoresis performed after SDS/bME denaturation in which proteins separate by size only.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Genetic technique in which a wild-type gene is introduced to rescue a mutant phenotype, used by Ohsumi to characterize Atg genes.

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Approach in which mutations are introduced genome-wide and survivors/defective lines are screened for a phenotype of interest (e.g., defective autophagy in yeast).

entries.subject: Biology — Cell Biology
entries.definition: Yeast mutants defective in secretion, used by Schekman to identify components of the secretory pathway.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: The number of entities (atoms, molecules, ions) per mole of substance. Bridges the microscopic and macroscopic worlds.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: The proportionality constant in the ideal gas law that relates pressure, volume, moles, and temperature. Appears in countless thermodynamic equations.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: The fundamental quantum of action relating photon energy to frequency. Underlies all quantum mechanics.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Planck's constant divided by 2π, used in angular-frequency forms of quantum equations.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: The speed of electromagnetic radiation in vacuum. Connects frequency and wavelength of photons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Relates temperature to average particle kinetic energy. The gas constant per molecule.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: The electric charge of one mole of electrons. Central to electrochemistry and relating charge to moles.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: The magnitude of electric charge on a single proton or electron.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: The mass of an electron at rest.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Rest mass of a proton.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Rest mass of a neutron; slightly larger than a proton's.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Ionization energy of hydrogen from the n=1 state; appears in Bohr-model transitions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Wavenumber form of the Rydberg constant used in spectral line equations.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Nominal acceleration of free fall at Earth's surface.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Relates total blackbody radiative power per unit area to the fourth power of absolute temperature.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: The electric constant in Coulomb's law; characterizes the ability of vacuum to permit electric field lines.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Standard Temperature and Pressure defined as 0 °C (273.15 K) and 100 kPa (1 bar). Used for reporting standard-state gas volumes.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Older STP convention still used in many US textbooks: 0 °C and 1 atm.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Standard Ambient Temperature and Pressure: 25 °C (298.15 K) and 100 kPa. Represents typical lab conditions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Reference conditions for tabulated thermodynamic data: 1 bar pressure for gases, 1 M for solutions, pure substance for solids/liquids.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: A non-SI unit of pressure based on average sea-level atmospheric pressure.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Pressure unit equal to 1/760 atm; mmHg is essentially equivalent.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: The SI unit of pressure: 1 N/m².

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Non-SI unit of pressure defined as exactly 100 kPa; essentially equal to 1 atm.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Energy unit, historically the heat to raise 1 g of water by 1 °C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Energy gained by an electron accelerated through 1 V. Common in spectroscopy and atomic physics.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Unit of PV-work commonly seen in gas calculations.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: SI base unit of absolute temperature. 0 K is absolute zero.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Temperature scale with 0 °C = freezing point, 100 °C = boiling point of water at 1 atm.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Length unit, commonly used for atomic distances and bond lengths.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: 1/12 the mass of a neutral ¹²C atom; used for atomic and molecular masses.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10⁹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10⁶.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10³.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10⁻².

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10⁻³.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10⁻⁶.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10⁻⁹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10⁻¹².

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Multiplier of 10⁻¹⁵.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: SI base unit for amount of substance; contains exactly 6.022 × 10²³ entities.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Concentration expressed as moles of solute per liter of solution.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Concentration expressed as moles of solute per kilogram of solvent. Temperature-independent.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Constants & Units
entries.definition: Ratio of moles of a component to total moles; dimensionless; sums to 1.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: The smallest unit of an element that retains its chemical identity; consists of a nucleus (protons + neutrons) and surrounding electrons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: A pure substance consisting of atoms with the same atomic number (number of protons).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Atoms of the same element differing in neutron count and thus mass number.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: The number of protons in a nucleus; uniquely identifies the element.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Total count of protons + neutrons in the nucleus.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Average mass of an element's atoms, weighted by natural isotopic abundance, in amu.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Mass of one mole of a substance; numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in g/mol.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: An atom or molecule with a net electric charge from losing or gaining electrons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Positively charged ion resulting from loss of electrons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Negatively charged ion from electron gain.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Tabular arrangement of elements by increasing atomic number grouped by similar chemical properties.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: A vertical column in the periodic table sharing valence-electron count and similar reactivity.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: A horizontal row of the periodic table corresponding to the principal quantum number n of the outer shell.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Electrons in the outermost shell; govern chemical bonding.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve eight valence electrons (stable noble-gas configuration).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Tendency of an atom in a bond to attract shared electrons. Pauling scale is most common. Increases up and right on the table; F is highest.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Energy needed to remove the most loosely bound electron from a gaseous atom or ion. Increases up and right on the table.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Energy released when a neutral atom gains an electron in the gas phase. Most negative for Cl.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Typical size of an atom; decreases across a period and increases down a group.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Net positive charge experienced by a valence electron after accounting for shielding by inner electrons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Group 1 elements (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr); very reactive, form +1 cations.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Group 2 elements (Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba, Ra); form +2 cations.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Group 17 elements (F, Cl, Br, I, At); highly electronegative nonmetals forming −1 anions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Group 18 elements; full valence shells make them nearly inert.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: d-block element capable of variable oxidation states and coordination complex formation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Rare-earth f-block elements; lanthanides = period 6, actinides = period 7 (mostly radioactive).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Wavefunction describing the probability distribution of an electron in an atom; characterized by quantum numbers n, ℓ, m_ℓ, m_s.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Spherically symmetric atomic orbital (ℓ=0); holds up to 2 electrons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Dumbbell-shaped atomic orbital (ℓ=1) with three orientations (p_x, p_y, p_z); set holds 6 electrons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Atomic orbitals with ℓ=2; five orientations; involved in transition-metal chemistry and expanded octets.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Atomic orbitals with ℓ=3; seven orientations; relevant for lanthanides/actinides.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Specifies electron shell and primary energy (n = 1, 2, 3…).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Specifies orbital shape (0=s,1=p,2=d,3=f); ranges 0 to n−1.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Specifies orbital orientation; ranges −ℓ to +ℓ.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Intrinsic angular momentum projection; ±½.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: No two electrons in an atom may share the same four quantum numbers; an orbital holds at most 2 paired electrons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Electrons fill degenerate orbitals singly with parallel spins before pairing.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Electrons fill atomic orbitals from lowest energy to highest.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Description of the distribution of electrons across orbitals (e.g., 1s² 2s² 2p⁶).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Early quantum model of the atom with electrons in fixed circular orbits; successful for H spectrum.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Wavelength associated with a moving particle: λ = h/p. Underlies wave-particle duality.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Position and momentum of a particle cannot be simultaneously known to arbitrary precision: Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Quantum of electromagnetic radiation; E = hν.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Periodic & Atomic
entries.definition: Ejection of electrons from a surface when illuminated by light of sufficient frequency; Einstein explained by photons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by complete electron transfer (metal + nonmetal).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Shared pair of electrons between two atoms; can be polar or nonpolar based on electronegativity difference.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Covalent bond with unequal electron sharing (ΔEN ≈ 0.4–1.7) producing a bond dipole.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Covalent bond with approximately equal sharing (ΔEN < ~0.4).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Delocalized "sea of electrons" shared across a lattice of metal cations; explains conductivity and malleability.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Bond formed by head-on orbital overlap along the internuclear axis; strongest covalent bond type and the first bond between two atoms.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Bond formed by side-by-side overlap of unhybridized p orbitals. Present in double and triple bonds, and restricts rotation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Bond consisting of 1 σ + 1 π; shorter and stronger than a single bond.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Bond consisting of 1 σ + 2 π; shortest and strongest between two given atoms.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Representation of a molecule showing bonding pairs, lone pairs, and formal charges.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: A valence electron pair localized on one atom, not shared in bonding; exerts more repulsion than bonding pairs.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Charge assigned to an atom in a Lewis structure: (valence e⁻) − (lone-pair e⁻) − ½(bonding e⁻).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Delocalization of π electrons represented by two or more contributing Lewis structures; actual structure is the weighted average.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion theory: molecular geometry arises from minimizing repulsion among electron domains around the central atom.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Each lone pair or bond (single, double, or triple counts as one domain) around a central atom in VSEPR.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Two electron domains arranged 180° apart (e.g., CO₂, BeCl₂).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Three electron domains at 120° in one plane (e.g., BF₃, SO₃).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Molecular shape from 3 or 4 electron domains with 1–2 lone pairs on central atom (e.g., SO₂ ~118°, H₂O ~104.5°).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Four bonded domains at 109.5° (e.g., CH₄, NH₄⁺).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Tetrahedral electron geometry with one lone pair; shape like NH₃ (~107°).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Five bonded domains with axial (90°) and equatorial (120°) positions (e.g., PCl₅).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry with one equatorial lone pair (e.g., SF₄).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry with two equatorial lone pairs (e.g., ClF₃, BrF₃).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Six bonded domains at 90° (e.g., SF₆).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Octahedral electron geometry with one lone pair (e.g., BrF₅).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Octahedral electron geometry with two trans lone pairs (e.g., XeF₄).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Mixing of atomic orbitals on the same atom to form equivalent hybrid orbitals that match molecular geometry.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: One s + one p orbital mixed; produces 2 linear hybrids at 180°. Typical of alkynes and cumulated dienes.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: s + 2p mixed; 3 trigonal hybrids at 120°; characteristic of alkenes, carbonyls, and aromatic carbons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: s + 3p mixed; 4 tetrahedral hybrids at 109.5°; typical of saturated carbons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Hybrid set of 5 orbitals for trigonal bipyramidal geometry; involves d-orbital participation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Hybrid set of 6 orbitals for octahedral geometry.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Bonding model in which atomic orbitals combine to form bonding and antibonding MOs delocalized over the molecule.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Half the difference of bonding and antibonding electrons; corresponds to number of bonds.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Highest Occupied and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbitals; govern reactivity and UV absorption.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Vector measure of bond or molecular polarity: µ = q·d; measured in debyes.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: A molecule is polar if it has polar bonds AND those bond dipoles don't cancel by symmetry. Lone pairs on the central atom almost always produce polarity.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Average distance between bonded atomic nuclei at equilibrium. Decreases with higher bond order.
entries.key_detail: C–C 154 pm, C=C 134 pm, C≡C 120 pm

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Energy required to break a bond homolytically in the gas phase. Always positive.
entries.key_detail: C–H ≈ 413 kJ/mol; C=O ≈ 745 kJ/mol; N≡N ≈ 941 kJ/mol

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Strong dipole-dipole attraction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on N, O, or F on another molecule. Responsible for water's high boiling point, DNA base pairing.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Electrostatic attraction between permanent molecular dipoles.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Weak, universal intermolecular attractions from instantaneous induced dipoles; strength grows with polarizability and size.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Attractive forces between molecules; order: ion-dipole > H-bond > dipole-dipole > dispersion.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: Central atoms in period 3+ can accommodate more than 8 valence electrons (e.g., PCl₅, SF₆) via d orbitals.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Bonding/VSEPR
entries.definition: LP–LP > LP–BP > BP–BP. Lone pairs compress adjacent bond angles by about 2–2.5°.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Energy is conserved; the internal energy change of a system equals heat added plus work done on it.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: The entropy of an isolated system always increases in a spontaneous process.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: The entropy of a perfect crystal at 0 K is zero, allowing absolute entropies to be defined.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Total energy contained within a thermodynamic system; a state function.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: State function H = U + PV; ΔH equals heat at constant pressure.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Measure of disorder/number of microstates; increases with T, volume of gas, moles of gas, mixing, and phase changes s→l→g.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: State function combining enthalpy and entropy; indicates spontaneity at constant T and P.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Process with ΔG < 0; spontaneous under the stated conditions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Process with ΔG > 0; non-spontaneous.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Releases heat; ΔH < 0.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Absorbs heat; ΔH > 0.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Relates standard free-energy change to the equilibrium constant.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Ratio of product to reactant activities with the same form as K but using current concentrations/pressures. Compare Q to K to predict shift.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Ratio of product to reactant activities at equilibrium; K_c uses concentrations, K_p uses pressures.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: When an equilibrium is disturbed by a change in concentration, pressure, or temperature, the system shifts to counteract the disturbance.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: The total enthalpy change of a reaction is the sum of ΔH for any sequence of steps summing to the same net reaction (path-independent).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Enthalpy change when 1 mol of a compound forms from its elements in their standard states. Zero for elements in standard states.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Free-energy change to form 1 mol of compound from elements in standard states.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Absolute molar entropy of a substance at standard state; always positive (third law).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Experimental measurement of heat transferred in a process, usually at constant P (coffee-cup) or constant V (bomb).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Heat required to raise 1 g of substance by 1 K.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Heat required to raise 1 mol of substance by 1 K (C_p at constant P, C_v at constant V).
entries.key_detail: C_p − C_v = R for ideal gas

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Enthalpy to melt 1 mol of solid at its melting point.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Enthalpy to vaporize 1 mol of liquid at its boiling point.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Relates vapor pressure to temperature using ΔH_vap.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant from ΔH°.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Mechanical work in expansion/compression against external pressure.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Property that depends only on current state, not path (e.g., U, H, S, G). In contrast, q and w are path functions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Transition between solid, liquid, gas (or between allotropes); at the phase-change temperature, q = n·ΔH_phase while T is constant.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: Energy transferred due to temperature difference; positive when added to system.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Thermodynamics
entries.definition: A process is spontaneous at constant T,P when ΔG < 0. Sign combinations of ΔH and ΔS determine temperature dependence.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Change in concentration of reactant or product per unit time.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Mathematical expression relating rate to concentrations: rate = k·[A]^m·[B]^n.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Proportionality constant in a rate law; depends on T (Arrhenius) and catalyst.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Exponent of each concentration in the rate law; overall order = sum of exponents. Determined experimentally.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Rate independent of reactant concentration; [A] decreases linearly with time.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Rate proportional to one concentration; exponential decay with constant half-life.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Rate proportional to [A]² (or [A][B]).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Time for reactant concentration to fall to half its initial value.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Rate-constant temperature dependence via activation energy.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Minimum energy barrier to convert reactants to products; height between reactant and transition state.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Highest-energy configuration on the reaction coordinate; not isolable. Marked by ‡.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Short-lived species formed and consumed during a reaction (e.g., carbocation, radical).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Species that increases reaction rate by providing a lower-Ea path without being consumed. Does not change ΔG or K.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Single-step reaction whose molecularity equals its order.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Number of molecules participating in an elementary step (uni-, bi-, termolecular).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Slowest step in a mechanism; sets the overall rate.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy and proper orientation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Assumes the concentration of a reactive intermediate remains constant during reaction.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Assumes a fast-reversible step reaches equilibrium before the slow step proceeds.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Plot of energy vs progress of reaction, showing transition states, intermediates, activation energies.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: The transition state resembles the species (reactant or product) closer to it in energy; applied to endothermic vs exothermic steps.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Kinetics
entries.definition: Kinetic product forms fastest (lower Ea); thermodynamic product is more stable (lower G). Varies with T and reversibility.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Species that increases [H⁺] in water.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Species that increases [OH⁻] in water.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Proton (H⁺) donor.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Proton (H⁺) acceptor.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Electron-pair acceptor (e.g., BF₃, AlCl₃, H⁺).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Electron-pair donor (e.g., NH₃, H₂O).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Species formed when a base accepts a proton.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Species left after an acid loses a proton; stronger acid → weaker conjugate base.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Negative base-10 log of [H₃O⁺]; neutral = 7 at 25 °C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Negative log of [OH⁻]; pH + pOH = 14 at 25 °C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Equilibrium constant for water autoionization.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Equilibrium constant for acid ionization in water.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Equilibrium constant for base protonation in water; Ka·Kb = Kw.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: −log Ka; lower pKa = stronger acid. Used to predict direction of acid-base equilibria (favor weaker acid).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Relates pH of a buffer to pKa and ratio of conjugate base to acid.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Solution of weak acid/conjugate base (or base/conj. acid) that resists pH change. Most effective within ±1 of pKa.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Controlled addition of titrant of known concentration to determine concentration of analyte.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Point in a titration where moles acid = moles base added (stoichiometric equivalence).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Very strong acid; pKa ≈ −10.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Perchloric acid; pKa ≈ −10. One of the strongest common acids.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Hydrobromic acid; pKa ≈ −9.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Hydrochloric acid; pKa ≈ −7.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Sulfuric acid first ionization; pKa ≈ −3.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Hydronium ion; pKa = −1.7. Sets a limit for acid strength in water (leveling effect).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Nitric acid; pKa ≈ −1.3.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Bisulfate; pKa ≈ 1.99 (second ionization of H₂SO₄).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Phosphoric acid first ionization; pKa ≈ 2.15.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Hydrofluoric acid; pKa ≈ 3.17. A weak acid despite F's electronegativity due to strong H–F bond.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Nitrous acid; pKa ≈ 3.40.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Canonical weak carboxylic acid; pKa = 4.76.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Carbonic acid first ionization; pKa ≈ 6.35. Important in blood buffering.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Hydrogen sulfide; pKa ≈ 7.0.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Dihydrogen phosphate (second H₃PO₄ ionization); pKa ≈ 7.20. Near physiological pH — good buffer.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Hypochlorous acid; pKa ≈ 7.53.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Ammonium; pKa ≈ 9.25.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Hydrogen cyanide; pKa ≈ 9.21.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Bicarbonate; pKa ≈ 10.33 (second ionization of H₂CO₃).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Hydrogen phosphate; pKa ≈ 12.35 (third ionization of phosphoric acid).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Auto-ionization pKa = 15.7; defines neutral reference.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Very strong organic acid; pKa −2 to 1. Much stronger than carboxylic acids.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Generic R-COOH; pKa 4–5.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa 9–11 for aliphatic amines.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa 10–11; more acidic than alcohols due to S size/polarizability.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ≈ 10; enhanced by resonance into the ring.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa 5–9; two flanking carbonyls strongly stabilize the enolate.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ~11 (e.g., ethyl acetoacetate).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ≈ 17.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa 19–20; key for enolate chemistry.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ≈ 25.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ≈ 25; acidic enough for NaNH₂ to deprotonate.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ≈ 36; conjugate base = NaNH₂ / amide ion.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ≈ 38.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ≈ 43.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ≈ 44.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: pKa ≈ 50 — essentially non-acidic. Conjugate base requires n-BuLi.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Strong base; conjugate acid H₂O (pKa 15.7). Deprotonates alcohols, carboxylic acids.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Moderately strong, non-nucleophilic base in EtOH; conjugate-acid pKa ≈ 16.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Strong, non-nucleophilic base; conjugate acid H₂ (pKa ~36).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Very strong base (conjugate acid NH₃, pKa 36). Deprotonates terminal alkynes.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Strong, bulky, non-nucleophilic base (pKa conj. acid ≈ 36). Used for kinetic enolates.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: Extremely strong, reactive base/nucleophile (pKa butane ≈ 50). Deprotonates weakly acidic C–H.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: σ-bond electron withdrawal/donation along a chain; electron-withdrawing groups stabilize conjugate bases and lower pKa.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: C–H acidity sp > sp² > sp³; more s-character → closer to nucleus → more stable carbanion.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Acid-Base/pKa
entries.definition: In water, all strong acids appear equally strong (leveled to H₃O⁺) and strong bases to OH⁻.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Loss of electrons / increase in oxidation state.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Gain of electrons / decrease in oxidation state.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Reaction involving simultaneous oxidation and reduction (electron transfer).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Formal charge on an atom assuming fully ionic bonds.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Electrochemical cell that generates electricity from a spontaneous redox reaction; ΔG < 0, E > 0.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Electrochemical cell that uses an external current to drive a non-spontaneous reaction.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Electrode where oxidation occurs. In galvanic cells, the negative terminal.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Electrode where reduction occurs. Positive terminal of a galvanic cell.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Device (e.g., KNO₃ gel) connecting half-cells that allows ion flow while preventing mixing; maintains electroneutrality.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Reference electrode (H₂/H⁺) with E° defined as exactly 0 V.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Tendency of a species to be reduced under standard conditions vs SHE; tabulated values.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Voltage produced by the cell: E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Relates free energy to cell potential.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Gives cell potential under non-standard concentrations.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Moles of substance produced at an electrode are proportional to charge passed: n = Q/(zF).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Use of electrical current to drive a non-spontaneous chemical change (e.g., water splitting, metal plating).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Spontaneous oxidation of metals (e.g., rusting of iron).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Electrochemistry
entries.definition: Galvanic cells packaged as a portable energy source (primary = disposable, secondary = rechargeable).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Equation of state for an ideal gas relating pressure, volume, moles, and temperature.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Point particles, no intermolecular forces, elastic collisions. Fails at high P and low T.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: At constant n,T: pressure and volume are inversely proportional.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: At constant n,P: volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: At constant n,V: pressure is directly proportional to absolute temperature.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: At constant P,T: volume is proportional to number of moles.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Combines Boyle, Charles, Gay-Lussac for fixed n.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Total pressure of a gas mixture equals the sum of partial pressures.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Correction for water vapor when collecting gas over water: subtract water vapor pressure at that T.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Rate of effusion is inversely proportional to √(molar mass). Lighter gases effuse faster.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Model explaining gas behavior from molecular motion and elastic collisions. Average translational KE is proportional to T.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Peak of the Maxwell–Boltzmann speed distribution.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Mean molecular speed in a gas.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Speed corresponding to the mean kinetic energy.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Probability distribution of molecular speeds in a gas; broadens and shifts right with T.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Real-gas equation correcting for molecular attraction (a) and finite volume (b).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Density of ideal gas from PV=nRT.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Gas Laws
entries.definition: Z = PV/nRT; equals 1 for ideal gas, deviates for real gases.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Saturated hydrocarbon with only C–C and C–H single bonds; CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ (acyclic). All sp³.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Hydrocarbon containing at least one C=C double bond; CₙH₂ₙ (acyclic). sp² carbons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Hydrocarbon containing at least one C≡C triple bond; sp carbons.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Functional group with hydroxyl attached to sp³ C. Primary, secondary, tertiary based on C classification.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Carbonyl with at least one H on the carbonyl carbon. C=O IR stretch ~1730 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Carbonyl with two alkyl/aryl groups and no H on C=O.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Has both C=O and O–H on the same carbon. Acidic (pKa 4–5).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Carboxylic acid derivative with alkoxy replacing OH. Sweet smell common.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Carboxylic acid derivative with N replacing OH. C=O conjugated with N lone pair (less reactive).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Nitrogen-containing group classified by degree of substitution (1°, 2°, 3°). Basic.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Two alkyl/aryl groups bonded to a single O. Low reactivity; common solvents.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Alkane with halogen (F, Cl, Br, I) substituent. Electrophilic at C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Sulfur analog of alcohol. More acidic (pKa ~10) and more nucleophilic than OH.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Sulfur analog of ether.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Bond between two sulfurs; stabilizes protein tertiary structure (cystine).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Aromatic ring with six π electrons delocalized around six sp² carbons; planar and highly stable.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Benzene ring bearing an OH. Acidic (pKa ~10) due to resonance stabilization of phenoxide.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Halogen attached directly to aromatic ring; much less reactive in SN reactions than alkyl halides.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Most reactive carboxylic acid derivative. C=O ~1800 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Two acyl groups sharing one oxygen. Two C=O bands at 1820 and 1760 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: C≡N triple bond. Sharp IR peak 2200–2260 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Strongly electron-withdrawing nitrogen group.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Carbon–nitrogen double bond; formed from aldehyde/ketone + primary amine.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Nitrogen α,β to a C=C bond; formed from ketone + secondary amine.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Tautomer of a carbonyl with C=C–OH; enolate is its conjugate base (O⁻). Nucleophilic at α-C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Carbon bonded to two OR groups; protecting group for carbonyls.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Carbon bonded to one OR and one OH; intermediate in acetal formation and sugar rings.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Three-membered ring with one oxygen; strained and electrophilic.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: C=O; present in aldehydes, ketones, esters, amides, carboxylic acids. δ+ on C, δ⁻ on O.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Alkane minus one H. Primary (1°), secondary (2°), tertiary (3°) based on the carbon bearing the attachment.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Smallest alkyl group; weakly electron-donating by hyperconjugation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Two-carbon alkyl group.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Ring-shaped saturated hydrocarbon (CₙH₂ₙ). Conformations include chair (cyclohexane).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Lowest-energy conformation of cyclohexane; alternating axial/equatorial C–H bonds.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: In chair cyclohexane, axial bonds are parallel to the ring's 3-fold axis; equatorial bonds point outward. Bulky substituents prefer equatorial.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: View down a C–C bond showing front-atom substituents (dot) and back-atom (circle); used to analyze rotational conformations.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Lowest-energy Newman geometry (substituents 60° apart).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Highest-energy Newman geometry (substituents aligned, 0°).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Staggered with two large groups 60° apart; strained by steric interactions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Staggered with two large groups 180° apart (most stable for butane).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Compounds with same molecular formula but different atomic connectivity.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Simplest alkane; tetrahedral, sp³.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Two-carbon alkane.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Three-carbon alkane.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Four-carbon alkane; exists as n-butane and isobutane.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Five-carbon alkane; three structural isomers.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Six-carbon alkane.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Seven-carbon alkane.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Eight-carbon alkane; reference in octane rating.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Nine-carbon alkane.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Ten-carbon alkane.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Systematic naming rules: longest parent chain, lowest locants for substituents, alphabetized substituents.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Number of rings plus π bonds in a molecule; useful for structure elucidation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: Cyclic, planar, fully conjugated molecule with (4n+2) π electrons (Hückel's rule); extra stability.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Functional Groups
entries.definition: A ring is aromatic if it has (4n+2) π electrons (n = 0,1,2…), is cyclic, planar, and fully conjugated. 4n π electrons = antiaromatic.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Unimolecular nucleophilic substitution via carbocation intermediate. Rate = k[substrate]. Favors 3° > 2° > 1° substrates; polar protic solvents; weak nucleophiles.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Bimolecular nucleophilic substitution; concerted backside attack causing inversion of stereochemistry. Rate = k[sub][Nu]. Favors 1° substrates; polar aprotic solvents; strong nucleophiles.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Unimolecular elimination via carbocation; rate = k[sub]. Forms Zaitsev alkene. Competes with SN1.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Bimolecular elimination; concerted anti-periplanar loss of H and LG. Strong base required. Zaitsev (or Hofmann with bulky base).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Elimination through a carbanion intermediate; favored when α-H is very acidic and LG is poor.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: In elimination, the more substituted (more stable) alkene is the major product.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Less-substituted alkene preferred when using bulky bases (e.g., t-BuOK) due to steric hindrance.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: H and leaving group must be 180° apart for E2; dictates stereochemistry of alkene product.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: In HX addition to alkenes, H adds to the carbon with more H's and X to the more substituted carbon (via more stable carbocation).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: H goes to more substituted carbon and X to less substituted; seen with HBr/peroxides (radical), hydroboration–oxidation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Addition of electrophile across a π bond; common for alkenes and alkynes.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Addition of water (H–OH) across a C=C. Acid-catalyzed gives Markovnikov; hydroboration–oxidation gives anti-Markovnikov.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Addition of H₂ across π bond with metal catalyst (Pd/C, Pt, Ni); syn addition.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Br₂ or Cl₂ adds anti across C=C via halonium ion. Gives vicinal dihalide.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: X₂ + H₂O across alkene; OH ends up on more substituted carbon (Markovnikov), X on less.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: BH₃ then H₂O₂/NaOH; syn anti-Markovnikov hydration of alkenes.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Hg(OAc)₂/H₂O then NaBH₄; Markovnikov hydration without rearrangement.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: m-CPBA converts alkene to epoxide in syn fashion.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: OsO₄ (or KMnO₄ cold) gives syn diol; ozonolysis cleaves.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: O₃ then Zn/H₂O (or Me₂S) cleaves C=C to two carbonyls; oxidative workup (H₂O₂) gives carboxylic acids.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Aromatic ring attacks an electrophile; H is replaced via σ-complex (arenium ion); rearomatization regenerates ring. Examples: halogenation, nitration, sulfonation, Friedel–Crafts.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Electron-donating groups (OH, OR, NR₂, alkyl) are ortho/para directors and activators; electron-withdrawing groups (NO₂, CN, CO, SO₃H) are meta directors and deactivators. Halogens are ortho/para directors but deactivators.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: RX + AlCl₃ → alkylarene. Suffers from polyalkylation and carbocation rearrangements.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: RCOCl + AlCl₃ → aryl ketone via acylium ion; no rearrangement.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: HNO₃/H₂SO₄ produces NO₂⁺ electrophile; installs −NO₂ on ring.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: SO₃/H₂SO₄ or fuming H₂SO₄ installs −SO₃H; reversible.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: [4+2] cycloaddition between a conjugated diene (s-cis) and a dienophile forming a cyclohexene. Concerted, suprafacial/suprafacial, stereospecific.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: In Diels–Alder, endo product (kinetic) has EWG oriented under the diene; exo is thermodynamic.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: X₂ + hν on alkane; selectivity: Br > Cl. Stability: 3° > 2° > 1° radicals; benzylic/allylic favored.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Three phases — initiation (form radicals), propagation (regenerate chain), termination (couple radicals).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: N-bromosuccinimide brominates allylic/benzylic C–H under low [Br₂] conditions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: RMgX with carbonyl → alcohol after workup. Formaldehyde gives 1°, aldehydes give 2°, ketones/esters give 3° alcohols.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: R-Li; even more reactive than Grignards. e.g., n-BuLi, PhLi.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Mild hydride reductant; reduces aldehydes/ketones to alcohols but not esters or acids.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Strong hydride reductant; reduces esters, carboxylic acids, amides, nitriles, and ketones to alcohols/amines.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Pyridinium chlorochromate oxidizes 1° alcohols to aldehydes (stops there) and 2° alcohols to ketones.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Strong oxidant; 1° alcohol → carboxylic acid, 2° alcohol → ketone.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: DMSO/oxalyl chloride/Et₃N; mild oxidation of alcohols to aldehydes/ketones.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Addition-elimination at a carbonyl bearing a leaving group (esters, acyl halides, anhydrides, amides). Reactivity: acyl halide > anhydride > ester ≈ acid > amide.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Acid + alcohol with H⁺ catalyst ⇌ ester + H₂O; equilibrium driven by excess reagent or water removal.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Base-catalyzed hydrolysis of ester to carboxylate + alcohol.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Enolate attacks carbonyl to give β-hydroxy carbonyl; dehydration gives α,β-unsaturated carbonyl.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Ester enolate + second ester → β-ketoester, using NaOEt.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: 1,4-conjugate addition of a stabilized nucleophile to an α,β-unsaturated carbonyl.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Phosphorus ylide (R₃P=CR'₂) + aldehyde/ketone → alkene + R₃P=O. Stereoselectivity depends on ylide stabilization.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Aldehyde/ketone + amine → imine, then reduced (NaBH₃CN, NaBH(OAc)₃) to amine.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: 1° amine + carbonyl → imine (Schiff base) + H₂O under mild acid.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: 2° amine + carbonyl → enamine + H₂O. Enamines act as soft nucleophiles (Stork enamine synthesis).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Terminal alkyne + NaNH₂ → acetylide, which attacks primary alkyl halide to extend chain.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: H₂/Lindlar catalyst (Pd/CaCO₃/Pb) gives cis alkene (partial reduction).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Na/NH₃(l) reduces alkyne to trans alkene via radical anions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Equilibrium between keto and enol forms via proton shift; keto generally favored.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Positively charged C⁺ intermediate; sp², planar. Stability: 3° > 2° > 1° > methyl; benzylic/allylic are especially stable by resonance.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: 1,2-hydride or 1,2-methyl shift to form a more stable carbocation. Diagnostic of SN1/E1 mechanisms.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Species donating electron pair to electrophile. Strength depends on basicity, polarizability, solvation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Electron-pair acceptor; attacks nucleophile.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Group that departs with bonding electrons. Good LGs are weak bases (I⁻, Br⁻, OTs, OMs).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Reaction Types
entries.definition: Temporarily masks a reactive functional group during a reaction (e.g., TBS ether for OH, acetal for carbonyl).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Isomers with same connectivity but different spatial arrangement of atoms.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Property of a molecule not superimposable on its mirror image. Usually due to a stereocenter.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Typically sp³ C bonded to four different groups.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Non-superimposable mirror images; identical physical properties except optical rotation and interactions with chiral environments.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Stereoisomers that are not mirror images; differ at some (but not all) stereocenters. Have different physical properties.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Molecule with stereocenters but an internal mirror plane → achiral overall.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: 1:1 mixture of enantiomers; no net optical rotation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Cahn-Ingold-Prelog priority rules; lowest-priority group points away; clockwise 1→2→3 = R, counterclockwise = S.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: For alkenes; Z (zusammen) when higher-priority groups are on the same side, E (entgegen) on opposite sides.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Simpler descriptor for disubstituted alkenes/rings; cis = same side, trans = opposite.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Rotation of plane-polarized light by a chiral compound; measured with polarimeter. (+) = dextrorotatory, (−) = levorotatory.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Standardized measure of optical rotation at specified wavelength (usually Na-D line), T, and concentration.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Percentage excess of one enantiomer: ee = (R−S)/(R+S) × 100%.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: 2D depiction: horizontal bonds project toward viewer, vertical away. Common for sugars and amino acids.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Historical system for sugars and amino acids based on Fischer projection; L amino acids have NH₃⁺ on the left.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Both groups add to the same face of a π bond (e.g., OsO₄, hydroboration, hydrogenation).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Groups add to opposite faces (e.g., Br₂, halohydrin, epoxide opening).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Different stereoisomers of starting material give different stereoisomer products (e.g., SN2, Diels–Alder).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Preferentially forms one stereoisomer over others (e.g., Markovnikov hydration, endo Diels–Alder).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Inversion of configuration at the electrophilic carbon during SN2 (umbrella flip).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Different arrangements around a single bond interconvertible by rotation (no bond breaking).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Interconversion of cyclohexane chair conformations; axial ↔ equatorial swap.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Energy preference (kcal/mol) for an equatorial vs axial substituent on cyclohexane.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Steric interaction between an axial substituent and axial H's on C3 and C5.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: A sp² or CH₂ center that can be converted to a chiral center by a single substitution; labeled pro-R/pro-S or Re/Si.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Stereochemistry
entries.definition: Separation of a racemic mixture; via chiral chromatography or diastereomer formation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Range of EM radiation from radio to γ-rays used in spectroscopy (UV-Vis, IR, NMR, etc.).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Absorbance is proportional to concentration and path length: A = ε·c·l.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Measures vibrational transitions of bonds; wavenumbers (cm⁻¹) map to functional groups. Fingerprint region < 1500 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Broad and strong at 3200–3600 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Medium, sharper than O–H; 3300–3500 cm⁻¹. Amines often show 1 or 2 bands depending on substitution.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Very broad and strong from 2500–3300 cm⁻¹; overlaps with C–H.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Absorption 2840–3000 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Alkene C–H 3050–3100 cm⁻¹; aromatic ~3030 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Strong, sharp near 3300 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 2100–2260 cm⁻¹, variable intensity.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Strong, sharp near 1730 cm⁻¹; conjugation lowers to ~1705.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 1715 cm⁻¹ (acyclic/6-membered); 1750 cm⁻¹ (5-membered); lower with conjugation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 1735 cm⁻¹ (saturated); 1715 with conjugation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 1710–1760 cm⁻¹; dimers lower, monomers higher.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: ~1690 cm⁻¹ (saturated).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Two bands 1820 and 1760 cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 1620–1680 cm⁻¹ (alkene); aromatic 1450–1600 cm⁻¹ (two bands near 1500 and 1600).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: IR region below 1500 cm⁻¹; unique to each compound but hard to assign individually.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Measures ¹H nuclear spin transitions in magnetic field. Chemical shift (ppm), integration, and splitting provide structural info.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Counts inequivalent carbons; sp³ ~0–50 ppm, sp² 100–160 ppm, C=O 160–220 ppm.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Position of NMR signal relative to TMS, reported in ppm. Depends on local electronic environment.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Area under an NMR peak is proportional to number of equivalent H's. Integration value of 3 often = methyl.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Neighboring (n) non-equivalent H's split a signal into n+1 lines (first-order, Pascal's triangle).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Spacing (Hz) between split peaks; reveals geometric relationship. Typical values: vicinal cis 6–14 Hz, vicinal trans 11–18 Hz, geminal 0–3 Hz.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Relative intensities of split multiplets: 1; 1,1; 1,2,1; 1,3,3,1; 1,4,6,4,1.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: (CH₃)₄Si; NMR internal reference at 0 ppm.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 0.5–2 ppm; methyls near 0.9, methylenes 1.2–1.5.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 2–3 ppm.

entries.term: NMR Shift — C-H next to O or N
entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 3–4 ppm for CH adjacent to electronegative atoms.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 5–6 ppm.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 6.5–8 ppm; integration tells number of ring H's.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 9–10 ppm; often a singlet or small doublet.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 11–13 ppm; often broad and exchangeable.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 5–10 ppm; variable, broad.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Ionizes a compound and separates by m/z; molecular ion (M⁺) gives MW. Isotope patterns reveal Cl (3:1 for M:M+2) and Br (1:1).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Radical cation formed on electron ionization corresponding to the intact molecule's mass.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Tallest peak in a mass spectrum; taken as 100% relative abundance.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Measures electronic transitions (π→π*, n→π*) in the 200–800 nm range. Used for conjugated systems and proteins.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 280 nm; ε ≈ 5500 M⁻¹·cm⁻¹. Dominant contributor to protein A₂₈₀.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: 257 nm; weak.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Absorbance at 280 nm dominated by Trp, Tyr. Estimates protein concentration via Beer's law.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Reciprocal wavelength (cm⁻¹); proportional to frequency and energy. Main x-axis in IR spectra.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: NMR multiplets with 0, 1, 2, 3 neighboring H's respectively (N+1 rule).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Spectroscopy
entries.definition: Distortionless Enhancement by Polarization Transfer; distinguishes CH, CH₂, CH₃ carbons by phase.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Molecule with central α-carbon bearing NH₃⁺, COO⁻, H, and variable R group. Proteinogenic AAs are L-configured (S at Cα, except cysteine).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Simplest amino acid; R = H. Achiral; fits tight turns; highly flexible.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Nonpolar aliphatic; R = CH₃.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Nonpolar branched; R = CH(CH₃)₂. Essential.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Nonpolar branched; R = CH₂CH(CH₃)₂. Essential.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Nonpolar branched; two stereocenters. Essential.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Nonpolar sulfur-containing (thioether); start codon amino acid. Essential.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Cyclic imino acid; side chain loops back to the nitrogen. Introduces kinks, disrupts α-helices.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Aromatic nonpolar; R = CH₂C₆H₅. Essential. UV absorption at 257 nm.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Aromatic with indole side chain. Essential. Dominant UV absorber at 280 nm.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Aromatic polar; para-hydroxy phenol side chain (pKa 10.07). Phosphorylation site.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Polar uncharged; R = CH₂OH. Phosphorylation site; active in serine proteases.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Polar uncharged; R = CH(OH)CH₃. Essential. Two stereocenters. Phosphorylation site.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Polar with –SH (pKa 8.33). Forms disulfide bonds (cystine).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Polar amide (amide form of aspartate).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Polar amide (amide form of glutamate).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Acidic; R = CH₂COO⁻ (pKa 3.65). Negatively charged at physiological pH.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Acidic; R = CH₂CH₂COO⁻ (pKa 4.25). Key neurotransmitter.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Basic; R = (CH₂)₄NH₃⁺ (pKa 10.53). Essential. Common post-translational modification site.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Basic with guanidinium side chain (pKa 12.48). Conditionally essential.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Imidazole side chain (pKa 6.00). Essential. Only AA with side-chain pKa near physiological pH — acts as proton shuttle in enzymes.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Cannot be synthesized by humans; must be obtained from diet. Mnemonic: PVT TIM HALL.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: pH where net charge on a molecule = 0. For AA with non-ionizable side chain, pI = (pKa₁ + pKa₂)/2.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Molecule with both positive and negative formal charges but zero net charge (e.g., amino acid at pI).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Amide linkage between α-COOH of one AA and α-NH₂ of next; planar, partial double-bond character.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Linear sequence of amino acids in a protein.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Local backbone folding patterns — α-helix and β-sheet — stabilized by H-bonds.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Right-handed helix with 3.6 residues per turn; backbone H-bond from C=O (i) to N-H (i+4). Disrupted by proline and glycine.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Extended strands laterally H-bonded; parallel or antiparallel.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: 3D folding of a single polypeptide stabilized by H-bonds, hydrophobic interactions, salt bridges, and disulfides.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits (e.g., hemoglobin tetramer).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Approximately 2.0–2.4.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Approximately 9.0–9.8.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: –S–S– formed by oxidation of two cysteine thiols; stabilizes tertiary/quaternary structure.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Nonpolar side chains (Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Met) buried inside folded proteins away from water.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Electrostatic interaction between a negatively charged (Asp/Glu) and positively charged (Lys/Arg) side chain.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Loss of 3D structure by heat, pH extremes, urea, or detergents, typically with loss of function.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Amino Acids & Proteins
entries.definition: Protein catalyst that lowers activation energy for a biochemical reaction; highly specific.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Homogeneous mixture of solute(s) dissolved in a solvent.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: The component of a solution present in largest amount (usually liquid).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Substance dissolved in a solvent.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Has O-H or N-H; H-bond donor (water, alcohols). Favors SN1/E1.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Polar but lacks O-H/N-H (DMSO, DMF, acetone, acetonitrile). Favors SN2 by not solvating nucleophile.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Properties depending on particle count, not identity: vapor-pressure lowering, boiling-point elevation, freezing-point depression, osmotic pressure.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: P_A = x_A·P°_A; partial pressure of a component above an ideal solution equals mole fraction times pure vapor pressure.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Solubility of a gas is proportional to its partial pressure: C = k·P.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: ΔT_f = K_f·m·i; solute lowers freezing point of solvent. Van't Hoff factor i accounts for dissociation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: ΔT_b = K_b·m·i.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Π = iMRT; pressure needed to prevent solvent flow across a semipermeable membrane.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Equilibrium constant for dissolution of a slightly soluble salt into its ions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Added common ion shifts solubility or dissociation equilibria (Le Châtelier).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Equilibrium involving multiple phases; pure solids/liquids omitted from K expression.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Quantitative relationships among reactants and products based on balanced equations and mole ratios.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Reactant fully consumed first; determines theoretical yield.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: (Actual yield / theoretical yield) × 100%.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound (e.g., CH₂O for glucose C₆H₁₂O₆).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Exact atom count in a molecule; integer multiple of empirical formula.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Burn sample, measure CO₂ and H₂O, back-calculate empirical formula.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Purification via dissolving solid in minimal hot solvent and crystallizing on cooling. "Like dissolves like" guides solvent choice.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Separation based on differential solubility in two immiscible solvents; acid/base extraction separates organic components by ionization.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Temperature where solid becomes liquid; pure samples have sharp ranges (~2 °C); impurities broaden and depress.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Purification by vaporization and recondensation; simple, fractional, or vacuum based on volatility differences.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Separation technique based on differential partitioning between stationary and mobile phases (TLC, column, GC, HPLC).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Silica plate; analytes visualized by UV, I₂, or stain. Rf = distance compound / distance solvent.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Retention factor in chromatography; 0–1, solvent-dependent.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Heating a reaction mixture at its boiling point with a condenser to prevent loss of volatile components.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Instrument that evaporates solvent under reduced pressure with rotating flask; standard for drying organic solutions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Anhydrous salt (Na₂SO₄, MgSO₄, CaCl₂) used to remove trace water from organic solvents.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Vacuum filtration setup; used after recrystallization to isolate crystals.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Common extraction solvent; b.p. 35 °C; volatile, flammable.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Common organic solvent; b.p. 40 °C; denser than water.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Common solvent; b.p. 78 °C; moderately polar.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Polar aprotic solvent; b.p. 56 °C. Widely used for cleaning glassware.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Polar protic solvent; b.p. 78 °C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
