entries.definition: Polar protic solvent; b.p. 65 °C. Toxic by ingestion.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Aromatic nonpolar solvent; b.p. 111 °C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Nonpolar hydrocarbon solvent; b.p. 69 °C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Ether solvent; b.p. 66 °C; coordinates cations (Grignard, organolithium solvent).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Polar aprotic solvent; high b.p. (189 °C); used in SN2 reactions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Polar aprotic solvent; b.p. 153 °C.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Universal polar protic solvent; b.p. 100 °C; uniquely strong H-bonding network.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Adsorbent used to remove colored impurities during recrystallization.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Fused bicyclic aromatic C₁₀H₈; common recrystallization unknown.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: C₆H₅COOH; simplest aromatic carboxylic acid (pKa 4.20).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Peracid reagent used for alkene epoxidation and sulfide→sulfoxide oxidation.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Palladium on carbon; heterogeneous hydrogenation catalyst.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Syn dihydroxylation reagent for alkenes; toxic.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Strong oxidant; cold dilute → syn diol; hot → cleavage to carboxylic acids.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Pd deactivated with Pb and quinoline; reduces alkyne to cis alkene only.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Sulfonate ester leaving groups; convert OH into a good leaving group for SN/E reactions.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Common drying agent for organic extracts; mild and easily filtered.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Saturated aqueous NaCl removes residual water from organic layer by osmotic partitioning.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Mild base; extracts carboxylic acids as sodium salts into aqueous layer without deprotonating phenols.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Strong acid used to protonate/ precipitate organic acids from aqueous solution during extraction.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Length of sample the light traverses; typically 1 cm in a standard cuvette.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Proportionality constant in Beer's law; characteristic of analyte at a given wavelength, units M⁻¹cm⁻¹.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Acid-catalyzed loss of water from an alcohol to give an alkene (E1 mechanism, Zaitsev).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Methyl ketone + X₂/OH⁻ → carboxylate + CHX₃ (chloroform/bromoform/iodoform).

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Organomagnesium compound; strong carbon nucleophile. Prepared from RX + Mg in anhydrous ether.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Ag(NH₃)₂⁺ reagent oxidizes aldehydes to carboxylates producing a silver mirror.

entries.subject: Chemistry — Other
entries.definition: Cu²⁺ reagent reduced by aldehydes to brick-red Cu₂O; distinguishes aldehydes from ketones.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A heritable unit of genetic information occupying a specific locus on a chromosome.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: An alternate form of a gene. Different alleles can produce different phenotypes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The specific allelic composition of a cell, either of the entire cell or, more commonly, for a certain gene or a set of genes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The observable properties of an organism produced by the genotype and its interaction with the environment.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A phenotype that is expressed in either the homozygous or heterozygous state. Usually represented with a capital letter.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A phenotype that is expressed only in the homozygous state. Usually represented with a lower-case letter.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A diploid organism having the same alleles at one or more genes; produces gametes of identical genotypes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A diploid organism having different alleles of one or more genes; produces gametes of different genotypes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Describes genes present in only one copy in an otherwise diploid organism, such as X-linked genes in a male.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A diagrammatic method for predicting the genotypes and phenotypes of offspring from a cross; assumes diploidy and Mendelian segregation.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: During gamete formation, the two alleles at a locus separate so each gamete receives only one; describes how alleles on homologous chromosomes separate during meiosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: During gamete formation, alleles of different (unlinked) genes segregate independently of one another.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: 19th-century monk (Experiments on Plant Hybrids, 1866) whose pea-plant crosses established the laws of segregation and independent assortment; foundation of classical genetics.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Garden pea, the organism Mendel used for his hybridization experiments.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The first filial generation — offspring from a cross of two parental (P) individuals.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The second filial generation, produced by crossing F1 individuals; Mendel's monohybrid F2 ratio was ~3:1.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A cross between individuals that differ at a single gene; yields a classic 3:1 phenotypic ratio in the F2.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A cross tracking two genes simultaneously; unlinked genes produce a 9:3:3:1 F2 phenotypic ratio.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A cross of an individual of unknown genotype to a homozygous recessive individual to reveal the unknown genotype.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Carl Correns (Germany), Hugo de Vries (Netherlands), and Erich von Tschermak (Austria) independently rediscovered Mendel's 1866 work in 1900.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The situation in which the heterozygote exhibits the phenotypes of both homozygotes (e.g., ABO blood types, AB).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: When one allele is not completely dominant so the heterozygote has an intermediate phenotype (e.g., pink snapdragons).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Existence of more than two allelic forms of a gene in a population (e.g., ABO blood system, Drosophila white).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Classic human multiple-allele/codominance example with alleles IA, IB, and i determining ABO blood type.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Disease caused by HbS allele of beta-globin; HbS/HbS is severe anemia with sickled RBCs, HbA/HbS heterozygotes show molecular codominance and malaria resistance (heterozygote advantage).
entries.sources: `2. Variations in Mendelian Inheritance.pptx`, `5. Population genetics.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Traits encoded by many loci acting additively (e.g., kernel color in wheat, human skin color).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: An allele that causes death of its carrier (often in homozygotes); can distort Mendelian ratios, e.g., 2:1 instead of 3:1.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The production, by one particular mutant gene, of apparently unrelated multiple effects at the phenotypic level.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The proportion of individuals with a specific genotype who manifest that genotype at the phenotypic level.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The degree to which a particular genotype is expressed in the phenotype (e.g., severity varies).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Ratio of the number of times an event is expected to happen over the number of opportunities; ranges from 0 (never) to 1 (always).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Probability of either one of two mutually exclusive events is the sum of their individual probabilities ("OR").

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Probability that independent events occur together is the product of their individual probabilities ("AND").

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A tree-like diagram used to enumerate gamete genotypes, zygote genotypes, or phenotypes from a cross.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A family tree diagram using standardized symbols to trace inheritance of a trait across generations.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Pattern in a pedigree in which affected individuals appear within a sibship but not across generations; suggestive of recessive inheritance.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Pattern in a pedigree where the trait appears in every generation; suggestive of dominant inheritance.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Rare, fatal, late-onset (~40s) degenerative neurological disease caused by a dominant allele; used as a pedigree example of dominant inheritance.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Statistical procedure that tests whether observed results differ significantly from expected; χ² = Σ (observed − expected)² / expected.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Assumption that there is no significant difference between observed and expected values.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The number of independently varying parameters in an experiment; for categorical genetics data, (number of classes − 1).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The tendency of genes located near each other on the same chromosome to be inherited together rather than assorting independently.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A group of genes on the same chromosome that travel together during inheritance.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The production of new allelic combinations in offspring due to crossing over between homologous chromosomes during meiosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Physical exchange of segments between homologous chromosomes during prophase I of meiosis; generates recombinant gametes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The cytological manifestation of crossing over — the X-shaped region where homologous chromatids have exchanged material during meiosis I.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Offspring carrying the same allele combinations as the parents; most abundant class when genes are linked.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Offspring carrying new allele combinations resulting from crossover between linked genes; the rarer classes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Measure of genetic distance; 1 cM equals 1% recombinants between two loci. Also called a map unit.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Drosophila geneticist who in 1910 established chromosomal basis of inheritance and sex linkage; Nobel 1933.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Fruit fly — a classic genetic model organism with short generation time, few chromosomes, and many mutants.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The most common allele in nature, often symbolized "+"; typically dominant if not specified.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Theory proposed by Walter Sutton and Theodor Boveri (1902) that genes reside on chromosomes; proved by Calvin Bridges (1916) via nondisjunction.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis, producing aneuploid gametes; used by Bridges as proof of the chromosomal theory.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Allele arrangement in a double heterozygote where both dominant (or both mutant) alleles are on the same chromosome (AB/ab).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Allele arrangement in a double heterozygote where the two dominant (or mutant) alleles are on different homologs (Ab/aB).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A cross used to measure genetic distance between two loci using recombination frequency.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A test cross using three linked genes that allows determination of gene order and map distances, and detection of double crossovers.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Two crossover events in a three-point cross; the DCO class is the least frequent and identifies the middle gene.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Offspring class resulting from a single exchange event in one map interval of a three-point cross.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Rare recombination between homologous chromosomes in somatic cells during mitosis; can be induced and used to create mosaic tissue.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The occurrence of cells of different genotypes within one individual (e.g., through mitotic recombination or X-inactivation).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Morgan's student; first to use recombination frequency to construct a linear genetic map.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Chromosomes other than the sex chromosomes; humans have 22 autosomal pairs.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Chromosomes that determine sex; in humans, pair 23 (X and Y).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A pictorial/ordered representation of all the chromosomes of a cell, typically arranged by size.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Larger human sex chromosome; carries ~1098 genes including those for hemophilia A, muscular dystrophy, and red/green colorblindness.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Smaller male-determining human sex chromosome; carries ~78 genes, most notably SRY.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Sex-determining Region Y gene; encodes a transcription factor that initiates male development in mammals.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The sex producing gametes with only one kind of sex chromosome; in humans, female (XX).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The sex producing gametes with two different kinds of sex chromosomes; in humans, male (XY).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The condensed, inactivated X chromosome in somatic cells of mammalian females (one X per cell is silenced).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Genes located on a specific sex chromosome; most commonly refers to X-linked.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: First sex-linked gene discovered, on the Drosophila X chromosome; white-eyed mutants studied by Morgan.
entries.sources: `3c. Sex chromosomes.pptx`, `2. Variations in Mendelian Inheritance.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: X-linked recessive bleeding disorder; classic example of sex-linked human inheritance.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: X-linked recessive muscle-wasting disease (Duchenne MD) caused by dystrophin gene mutations.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: X-linked recessive vision defect affecting opsin genes, commonly cited sex-linked human trait.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Sex-determination system in birds, some fishes, and moths in which females are the heterogametic sex (ZW) and males are ZZ.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Ratio of X chromosomes to autosome sets that determines sex in Drosophila; ≥1.0 female, ≤0.5 male, ~0.67 intersex.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Traits expressed in both sexes but differently affected by sex (e.g., male pattern baldness frequency differs).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A genetically controlled character phenotypically exhibited in only one of the two sexes (e.g., milk production).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Classic sex-influenced human trait, expressed more strongly in males.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Single-cell genetic sex-determination system; yeast has **a** and **α** mating types, Chlamydomonas has + and −.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Gene interaction where one gene masks or modifies the phenotypic expression of another, non-allelic gene; usually produces <4 phenotypes in a dihybrid cross.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Two genes required in dominant form for a single phenotype; dihybrid ratio is 9:7 (e.g., sweet pea flower color).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Homozygous recessive at one gene masks expression of the second gene; dihybrid ratio 9:3:4 (e.g., mouse coat color agouti).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A dominant allele at one locus masks expression of alleles at a second locus; dihybrid ratio 12:3:1 (e.g., summer squash color).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A dominant allele at one locus suppresses the expression of a dominant allele at another; dihybrid ratio 13:3 (e.g., chicken feather color).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Two genes that produce the same phenotype; a dominant allele at either locus gives the trait, giving a 15:1 dihybrid ratio.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Production of a wild-type phenotype when two recessive mutations in DIFFERENT genes are combined in a diploid or heterokaryon.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Test developed by E. Lewis to determine whether two independent mutations are within the same cistron (gene); failure to complement = allelic.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A set of mutations that fail to complement each other, representing one gene (cistron).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A DNA sequence defining one polypeptide / one complementation unit; often synonymous with gene.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: In complementation, mutations behaving as allelic — they must be on the same chromosome to work. Also: regulatory elements acting on nearby genes on same DNA.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mutations (or regulatory products) that can complement each other across the two homologs in a heterozygote; usually diffusible products.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A cell containing two or more genetically different nuclei in a common cytoplasm, used in complementation tests in fungi.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The state in which a genetic screen has recovered multiple alleles of every gene in a pathway; assessed by the frequency distribution of complementation groups.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A mutant in which a region is deleted, so it fails to complement any mutation within the deleted interval; useful for mapping.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Earliest developmental stage of the fly after fertilization, undergoing rapid nuclear divisions to form a syncytial, then cellular, blastoderm.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Stage in Drosophila when cell membranes form around the peripheral nuclei of the syncytial blastoderm.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Process of cell movements in the early embryo that forms the three germ layers.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Groups of cells in the Drosophila larva that differentiate during pupation into adult structures (e.g., eye, wing, leg discs).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Feeding immature stage of Drosophila development preceding the pupa.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Non-feeding, metamorphic stage of Drosophila in which imaginal discs differentiate into adult structures.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Multiply inverted chromosome (e.g., SM1, TM2) that suppresses viable recombination and carries a dominant marker and recessive lethal; used to maintain lethal/sterile mutant stocks.
entries.sources: `4a. Devo Intro.pptx`, `8a. Fly and Mouse intro.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Stock containing two different homozygous-lethal chromosomes that stably propagate heterozygotes; used in Drosophila genetic screens.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Dominant curly-wing marker on the SM1 balancer chromosome in Drosophila; homozygous lethal.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Winners of the 1995 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for their genetic dissection of Drosophila early development.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Gene whose mRNA or protein is deposited by the mother into the egg; the offspring phenotype depends on the maternal genotype. Earliest developmental genes in Drosophila.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Drosophila maternal-effect anterior morphogen; its mRNA is tethered to the minus end of microtubules at the anterior pole. Bicoid protein is a transcription factor that activates hunchback and a translational inhibitor of caudal.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Drosophila posterior maternal-effect gene; nos mRNA is localized at the posterior (plus-end of microtubules) and its protein is a translational repressor of maternal hunchback mRNA.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Substance that defines different cell fates in a concentration-dependent manner; Bicoid was the first molecularly described morphogen.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Zygotic gap gene; transcription is initiated anteriorly by Bicoid and also regulated by Nanos translational repression of maternal hb.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Posteriorly acting transcription factor whose maternal mRNA translation is inhibited anteriorly by Bicoid.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The body axis distinguishing back from belly; in Drosophila established by Spaetzle/Toll signaling in follicle cells on the ventral side.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Transmembrane receptor that mediates dorsal-ventral patterning in Drosophila after activation by Spaetzle; homologous family (TLRs) has roles in innate immunity.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Genes first transcribed in the zygote that refine anterior/posterior patterning into segments; subdivided into gap, pair-rule, and segment-polarity classes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: First zygotic segmentation genes expressed; mutations produce gaps in segmentation pattern. Encode transcription factors.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Zygotic segmentation genes whose mutations delete pattern elements from every alternate segment; sharpen segmental boundaries.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Zygotic segmentation genes expressed in stripes with single-segment periodicity; mutations delete part of each segment and mirror-duplicate the remainder. Example: engrailed.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Drosophila segment-polarity gene expressed in stripes in the posterior compartment of each segment.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Selector genes that assign a unique identity to each segment; regulated by gap, pair-rule, and segment-polarity genes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A 60-amino-acid (180-bp) DNA-binding motif (helix-turn-helix) present in many homeotic genes; binds 8 bp upstream of target genes and is highly conserved.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: One of two Drosophila homeotic gene clusters; mutations convert thoracic/abdominal identities (e.g., extra wings in bithorax).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: One of two Drosophila homeotic gene clusters; dominant gain-of-function converts antennae into legs.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Conserved clustered homeobox genes that specify anterior-posterior identity across animals; ordered colinearly with their expression domains.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Master regulator gene for eye development; conserved from fly (eyeless) to mammals (Pax-6); ectopic expression induces eyes on limbs.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Drosophila receptor tyrosine kinase required for formation of photoreceptor R7; mutants lack R7.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Ligand on the neighboring R8 photoreceptor that activates sevenless on R7, triggering R7 differentiation.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Drosophila late-onset mutant showing adult lethality; example of a late-onset neurodegenerative phenotype.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Nematode model organism with invariant cell lineage and transparent body; used to study development and apoptosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: C. elegans vulva-defective mutant phenotype in which eggs hatch inside the hermaphrodite.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The study of allele and genotype frequencies in populations and how they change under evolutionary forces.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The proportion of individuals with a specific genotype in a population; sum of all genotypic frequencies equals 1.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The proportion of all alleles of a gene in a population that are of a specific type; for two alleles, p + q = 1.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Both allele and genotype frequencies remain constant from generation to generation unless disturbing influences (selection, drift, mutation, migration, non-random mating) act.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: (1) No natural selection, (2) random mating, (3) no mutation, (4) no migration, (5) large population (no genetic drift).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Expansion for three alleles: (p + q + r)² = p² + 2pq + q² + 2pr + 2qr + r².

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Differential reproductive success of genotypes; drives changes in allele frequency across generations.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Random changes in allele frequencies due to sampling in small populations.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Movement of alleles into or out of a population; disturbs Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Directional selection example in peppered moth (Biston betularia): dark morph rose in frequency during industrial pollution of tree bark.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Selection favoring heterozygotes over both homozygotes; classic example is HbA/HbS heterozygotes having malaria resistance.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Selection that favors the intermediate phenotypes and reduces variance.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Selection favoring one extreme phenotype, shifting allele frequencies over time.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Selection favoring both extreme phenotypes over the intermediate, potentially leading to speciation.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Naturalist (1809–1882); proposed natural selection as the mechanism of evolution in "On the Origin of Species" (1859).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Godfrey H. Hardy (mathematician) and Wilhelm Weinberg (physician) who independently derived the population-genetic equilibrium principle in 1908.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Bacterial strain able to grow on minimal medium (no supplements required).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A mutant bacterial strain requiring one or more additional nutrients in the medium to grow (auxilium = help), e.g., met-, bio-, thr-, leu-.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Medium containing only the minimum nutrients needed for wild-type (prototrophic) growth; used to score auxotrophy.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: 1943 fluctuation test showing that bacterial mutations (to phage resistance) arise spontaneously, not in response to selection.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Demonstrated genetic exchange (conjugation) between E. coli auxotrophs at ~1 in 10⁷ frequency.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Unidirectional DNA transfer via direct cell contact from a donor (male) to a recipient (female) bacterial cell.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Uptake of naked DNA from the environment by a bacterial cell. Shown by Griffith (1928) and Avery-MacLeod-McCarty (1944) to prove DNA is the genetic material.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Transfer of bacterial DNA from donor to recipient inside a bacteriophage particle.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Extrachromosomal circular double-stranded DNA element that replicates autonomously from the host chromosome.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: ~100 kb episome that confers donor "maleness" on E. coli; carries tra genes and a transfer origin.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: E. coli cell containing a free F plasmid; acts as a conjugation donor.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: E. coli cell lacking the F plasmid; acts as the conjugation recipient.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: "High frequency of recombination" strain — F plasmid has integrated into the bacterial chromosome; during conjugation transfers chromosomal genes in a time-dependent manner.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: F plasmid that has excised from an Hfr chromosome carrying a piece of host chromosomal DNA; allows partial diploid analysis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Experimental technique that halts conjugation at intervals to determine the order and timing of gene transfer from Hfr to F−; produces a linear map in minutes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Protein filament on donor bacterial surface that initiates contact with the recipient during conjugation.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: 1928 Pneumococcus experiment showing that a "transforming principle" from heat-killed smooth bacteria could convert rough bacteria to virulent smooth; early evidence for DNA as genetic material.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: 1944 experiment identifying DNA (not protein, lipid, or RNA) as the molecule responsible for transformation in Pneumococcus.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A virus that infects bacteria.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A clear area on a bacterial lawn where phage have lysed the cells; each plaque originates from a single phage particle.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Phage that always enters the lytic cycle, rapidly multiplies, and kills the host.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Phage that can enter either the lytic or the lysogenic cycle.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Phage life cycle in which the phage replicates, assembles, and lyses the host cell to release progeny.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Phage life cycle in which the phage genome integrates into the host chromosome and is passively replicated.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Integration of bacteriophage DNA into the host chromosome as a prophage.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The phage genome when integrated into the bacterial chromosome during lysogeny.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Type of transduction in which any random piece of the bacterial chromosome can be packaged in a phage head and transferred; mediated by P1, P22.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Transduction in which only bacterial DNA immediately adjacent to the prophage integration site is transferred (e.g., phage lambda).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Salmonella phage that integrates into the bacterial chromosome; first used in generalized transduction.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: E. coli phage used in generalized transduction; does not integrate but replicates as a plasmid and encodes Cre recombinase with loxP sites.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Temperate E. coli phage; workhorse for molecular biology. Uses the cI/cII/cro lysis–lysogeny switch and performs specialized transduction.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Phage lambda repressor that maintains lysogeny by blocking lytic gene expression.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Lambda phage regulator that activates cI expression and biases the initial lysis-vs-lysogeny decision toward lysogeny.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Lambda phage regulator that blocks cI expression, pushing the switch toward lysis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Protein that allows RNA polymerase to read through transcription termination signals; in lambda, controls lytic gene expression.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Average number of phage particles infecting each bacterial cell. Two or more phage per cell is required for viral recombination.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Set of E. coli bacteriophages (T1–T7); T-even phages (T2, T4) were classical genetic tools.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: T2 gene with alleles h+ (wild-type, infects E. coli B only — cloudy plaque) and h− (infects B and B/2 — clear plaque).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: T2 gene: r+ gives small plaques; r− (rapid lysis) gives large plaques.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Geneticist who used T4 rII mutants for fine-structure intragenic mapping, resolving recombination within a single gene.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: T4 phage locus famously mapped in fine detail by Benzer; showed the gene is divisible into many recombinable sites.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Retrovirus discovered by Peyton Rous (1910) that causes sarcomas in chickens; carries the v-src oncogene.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Three structural genes of retroviruses: gag (capsid proteins), pol (reverse transcriptase, integrase), env (envelope glycoprotein).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The actions or reactions of organisms under specified circumstances — can be simple (chemotaxis) to complex (mating).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Directed movement of cells toward an attractant; in E. coli, controlled by flagellar rotation (CCW = run, CW = tumble).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Comparative studies of monozygotic (identical) and dizygotic (fraternal) twins to partition genetic vs. environmental contribution to traits.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Proportion of twin pairs in which both members share a trait; higher concordance in MZ than DZ twins suggests a genetic component.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Twin pairs in which only one twin has the trait.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Drosophila gene isolated 1963; transcription factor controlling male courtship behavior. Sex-specific splicing produces male- and female-specific proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Accessory olfactory organ mediating pheromonal/social behavior in mammals; signals through TRP2.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Transient Receptor Potential cation channel localized to VNO sensory microvilli; required for VNO chemosensation.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A physiological cycle of about 24 hours present in all eukaryotes that persists in the absence of external cues.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: First clock gene isolated (in Drosophila, on the X chromosome). Period protein heterodimerizes with Timeless and feeds back to inhibit its own transcription.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Drosophila clock gene whose product partners with Period; broken down by light.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Transcription-factor pair that activates per and tim expression; target of Per/Tim negative feedback.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Hypothalamic nucleus acting as the master mammalian circadian pacemaker; secretes signaling molecules (e.g., TGF-α) to control activity.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Photopigment in intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) that entrains circadian rhythms to light.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells containing melanopsin; transmit light cues to the SCN.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Regulatory circuit in which clock proteins negatively regulate their own transcription, producing ~24-hour oscillations.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Inheritance of genes carried in cytoplasmic organelles (mitochondria, chloroplasts) or cytoplasmic microbes, typically uniparentally (maternal in animals).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Inheritance from only one parent; in animals typically maternal (mitochondrial), since sperm mitochondria are eliminated.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Small circular genome in mitochondria (human: 16.6 kb, 37 genes; 13 proteins plus rRNAs/tRNAs); uses a slightly different genetic code.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Circular genome in chloroplasts encoding rRNAs, tRNAs, and many photosynthetic proteins (~120 genes in M. polymorpha).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Coexistence of mutant and wild-type mtDNA in the same cell; the proportion of mutant mtDNA determines severity of disease.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Organism (e.g., yeast) that can grow with or without oxygen; S. cerevisiae grown on glucose can survive mitochondrial mutations.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Single-celled green alga ("green yeast") used as a genetic model for chloroplast inheritance.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The most recent matrilineal common ancestor of all living humans, inferred from mtDNA diversity.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Use of sequence divergence (e.g., in mtDNA) to estimate time since common ancestry.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Post-transcriptional modification of RNA sequence (e.g., insertion/deletion of U residues in trypanosome mitochondrial mRNAs).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Protist with the most gene-rich known mitochondrial genome; cited as limit case for mtDNA complexity.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The failure of cells to remain constrained in their growth properties, giving rise to tumors.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A tumor that grows to a certain size and then stops, usually with a fibrous capsule; does not invade.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A tumor whose cells can invade surrounding tissues and spread to other organs.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Spread of malignant tumor cells through the body to establish new tumors at distant sites.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Pharmacological cancer treatment, often targeting S-phase cells (e.g., Ara-C, 6-azauracil inhibit DNA synthesis).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Cytosine arabinoside; nucleoside analog with arabinose instead of ribose that terminates DNA chain elongation.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Chemotherapy drug that inhibits OMP decarboxylase, blocking pyrimidine synthesis and DNA replication.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A gene whose action promotes cell proliferation; oncogenes act dominantly (gain of function) to cause cancer.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Normal cellular gene that regulates cell proliferation and that can be converted by mutation into a cancer-promoting oncogene.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Rous Sarcoma Virus oncogene encoding a protein tyrosine kinase; v-src lacks the C-terminal inhibitory phosphorylation site and is constitutively active.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: An oncogene captured by a retrovirus from a cellular proto-oncogene; acquired by reverse transcription of cellular mRNA into the viral genome.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Four functional categories: growth factors, growth-factor receptors, intracellular signal transducers (e.g., Ras), and nuclear transcription factors (e.g., Myc).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Intracellular GTPase proto-oncogene; mutant activated Ras is a common oncogene in human cancers.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Nuclear transcription factor proto-oncogene; deregulated expression drives proliferation and contributes to many cancers.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Gene that normally restrains cell proliferation; cancer results when BOTH copies are inactivated (recessive at the cell level, loss of function).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Tumor-suppressor transcription factor (53 kDa), mutated in over 50% of human cancers; stabilized by DNA damage, induces cell-cycle arrest (p21) and apoptosis (BAX, FAS).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor transcriptionally induced by p53; arrests the cell cycle in response to DNA damage.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Classic tumor suppressor; Rb protein reversibly inhibits cell-cycle progression. Loss of both alleles causes childhood retinal tumors.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Form of retinoblastoma in which one mutant Rb allele is inherited; a single somatic hit inactivates the second allele causing early, often bilateral tumors.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Non-inherited form requiring two independent somatic hits in Rb; typically late, unilateral.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Alfred Knudson's model (from retinoblastoma) that cancer from a tumor suppressor requires both alleles be inactivated ("two hits").

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Tumor suppressor gene whose loss initiates colorectal polyps and is an early step in colon cancer progression.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Concept that cancer arises through a sequence of genetic lesions (multiple oncogene activations and tumor suppressor losses) accumulating over time.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Fusion genes produced by chromosomal translocations t(2;13) or t(1;13) that drive alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma by fusing Pax DNA-binding domain to Forkhead transactivation domain.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Budding/baker's yeast — eukaryotic model organism; propagates as haploid or diploid; genome ~5885 genes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Fission yeast; eukaryotic model with a single Cdk protein (Cdc2).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Standard prokaryotic model organism; haploid, circular ~4,639 kb genome, ~4288 genes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Small nematode model organism; transparent, invariant cell lineage (959 somatic cells in hermaphrodite), useful for development and apoptosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mouse — mammalian genetic model; ~21,000 genes; 6–9 week generation time; extensive transgenic and knockout toolkit.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Small flowering plant model organism; ~25,000 genes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Drosophila DNA transposon engineered as a transformation vector; used to insert transgenes. Transposase is typically supplied in trans to keep inserts stable.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Reduced-size white+ gene used as an eye-color selectable marker in Drosophila transformation constructs.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Regulatory sequence just upstream of a gene that is the binding site for RNA polymerase.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Cis-acting regulatory sequence that can elevate transcription from a promoter; may act over tens of kb and be 5′, 3′, or intronic.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Yeast-derived two-component gene-expression system: a tissue-specific GAL4 "driver" activates transcription of a target gene placed downstream of UAS sequences.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Yeast regulatory element bound by GAL4 transcription factor; used in Drosophila GAL4/UAS binary expression.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: RNA interference — uses antisense / double-stranded RNA to block mRNA stability or translation, creating loss-of-function "knockdowns."

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Engineered integration site in the genome (e.g., φC31 attP) that allows site-specific insertion of new transgenes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Site-specific recombinase derived from Streptomyces phage φC31; catalyzes integration at attP/attB sites, used for targeted Drosophila transgenesis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Any individual carrying foreign DNA inserted into the genome of its organism.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Totipotent cells from the inner cell mass of an early mammalian embryo; can be cultured, genetically modified, and injected into a blastocyst to produce transgenic / knockout mice.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Capable of giving rise to every cell type of the embryo and adult.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Transgenic mouse in which a specific gene has been inactivated, usually by homologous recombination using a targeting vector in ES cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Construct that replaces a target gene coding region with a selectable marker (neoR) while flanking with homology arms; often includes negative selection (HSV-tk) outside homology arms.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Positive selection system: neomycin-resistance gene allows cells that took up the construct to survive in G418 (neomycin analog).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Negative selection: Herpes Simplex thymidine kinase converts ganciclovir (GCV) to a DNA-chain-terminating nucleotide; kills cells in which the construct integrated non-homologously.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Zn-inducible promoter used to drive transgene expression (e.g., rat growth hormone in the "giant mouse" experiment).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Growth/differentiation factor that limits muscle growth; knockout mice develop large muscles.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Enzyme that maintains telomeres by adding hexameric repeats; knockouts develop premature aging phenotypes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Premature-aging disease caused by Lamin A mutations; modeled in telomerase-deficient mice.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Site-specific recombinase from phage P1 that catalyzes recombination between 34 bp loxP sequences; central tool for conditional genetics.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: 34 bp recognition sequence for Cre recombinase; two loxP sites flanking DNA allow Cre-mediated excision.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: To flank a gene of interest with loxP sites so it can be excised by Cre.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Gene inactivation restricted to a particular tissue, achieved by mating a Cre driver (expressing Cre tissue-specifically) with a floxed allele strain.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Conditional activation/expression of a transgene only in a given tissue, often using a floxed transcriptional STOP cassette that is removed by Cre.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats; bacterial adaptive immune system repurposed for genome editing in any organism.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: CRISPR-associated Protein 9 (from Streptococcus pyogenes); an RNA-guided DNA endonuclease used in genome editing.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Short RNA that directs Cas9 to a complementary DNA target for cleavage.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Error-prone DNA double-strand break repair pathway; after CRISPR cleavage usually produces indels that knock out the gene.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: DSB repair using a homologous template; used with CRISPR to introduce precise edits/knock-ins.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Fragment of foreign (viral/plasmid) DNA captured into a bacterial CRISPR array to guide future defense.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Identification of a wild-type gene by its ability to restore (rescue) a mutant phenotype — via mRNA/protein/DNA introduction.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mutation whose phenotype depends on an environmental condition (e.g., temperature-sensitive), allowing study of essential genes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Conditional mutation producing wild-type protein at permissive temperature but inactive protein at restrictive temperature.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Technique of transferring colonies from one plate to others to test growth under different conditions (e.g., temperature, auxotrophy).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A DNA segment that can move from one location in the genome to another ("jumping genes"); often flanked by repeats.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Maize geneticist who discovered controlling elements (Ds/Ac) in the 1940s; Nobel Prize 1983.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: "Dissociation" — McClintock's transposable element in maize that causes chromosome breakage at its insertion site.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Small bacterial transposable element carrying only the genes needed for its own mobility; flanked by inverted repeats.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Identical sequences in reverse orientation at the ends of transposons, recognized by transposase for mobilization.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Enzyme encoded by a transposon that catalyzes its excision and re-insertion into the genome.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mobile DNA element that moves via a "cut and paste" or replicative mechanism using transposase.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Transposon composed of two IS elements flanking additional genes (e.g., antibiotic resistance).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mechanism in which the transposon is copied and the copy inserts at the new site, leaving the original in place.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Non-replicative mechanism in which the transposon is excised from one site and inserted at another.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mobile element that transposes via an RNA intermediate and reverse transcriptase; cannot self-excise.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Non-retroviral retrotransposon, 6–8 kbp; encodes reverse transcriptase/endonuclease. Humans have ~850,000 LINEs, ~50 functional (e.g., L1).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Short retrotransposon lacking its own reverse transcriptase; mobilized by LINE enzymes. Includes the Alu family.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The most abundant human SINE (~300 bp) derived from 7SL RNA; >1 million copies in the human genome.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Temperate E. coli bacteriophage that replicates as a transposon; 37 kb linear genome; can cause deletions and inversions upon transposition.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Phenomenon in Drosophila where P+ male × P− female crosses produce sterile offspring due to uncontrolled P element mobilization in the germline.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Use of transposons to disrupt and thereby tag genes for identification.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The sum total of genetic information in a cell or organism.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The amount of DNA per haploid genome; varies widely and not strictly correlated with organismal complexity.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Observation that genome size does not correlate well with organismal complexity, partly due to non-coding and repetitive DNA.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Classical chain-termination DNA sequencing method using dideoxynucleotides; performed with capillary gel electrophoresis and fluorescent dyes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Strategy of first creating a low-resolution physical map of a genome to guide ordered sequencing of clones.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Sequencing random clones of a genome and assembling overlaps computationally, bypassing prior mapping.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Bacteriophage whose 5386 bp, 11-protein genome was the first ever sequenced (Sanger group, 1977).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Bacterium (~1743 genes) — first free-living organism to have its complete genome sequenced (1995).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Bacterium with one of the smallest known cellular genomes (~470 genes); benchmark for minimal-genome studies.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Homologous genes in different species that arose from the same gene in the common ancestor.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Homologous genes within a single species that arose by gene duplication.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Similarity between sequences or structures due to inheritance from a common ancestor.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: State in which two or more homologous loci are found linked together in several species, often used in comparative genomics.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Leader of the private (Celera) human genome sequencing effort; pioneer of shotgun and synthetic genomics.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Leader of the public Human Genome Project consortium.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Diploid nuclear genome of Homo sapiens: ~3 billion bp, ~21,000 genes, ~1% coding exons, >50% repeats, ~40% with unknown function.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Standard computational algorithm used to predict gene structures in genomic DNA.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man — curated database of human genes and genetic disorders.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Large blocks of duplicated genomic sequence; ~1077 blocks in the human genome.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Region of tandem repeats on each chromosome (human repeat ~171 bp spanning megabases); where kinetochores form.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Hexameric tandem repeat at chromosome ends (5–10 kb in humans); protects the ends from erosion.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Tandem array of ribosomal RNA genes (18S, 5.8S, 28S) present on acrocentric chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21, 22; separated by intergenic spacers.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Single-Nucleotide Polymorphism — single base pair substitution; simplest and most abundant class of DNA polymorphism.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: DNA element composed of 5–100 bp tandem repeats (Variable Number Tandem Repeats); codominant and useful in genetic fingerprinting.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: DNA element composed of 15–100 tandem repeats of short (2–5 bp) sequences; highly polymorphic.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Variation at a locus within a population; much greater within human populations than between them.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The smallest set of genes required to support cellular life; ~256–480 genes inferred by comparing M. genitalium and H. influenzae.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Organism able to grow on only CO₂, light, and inorganic salts; requires the largest genome of metabolic categories.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Organism requiring an organic carbon source (e.g., glucose) plus inorganic salts.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Organism that can only replicate inside a host cell; typically has highly reduced genomes (e.g., Mycoplasma).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: A genome produced de novo in the laboratory by chemical synthesis and assembly; examples include synthetic poliovirus and synthetic Mycoplasma.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: First synthetic viral genome: overlapping 69-bp oligos assembled into 400–600 bp segments, ligated, transcribed in vitro, and added to HeLa extract to generate infectious virions.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Transferring a complete genome (synthetic or natural) into a recipient cell that then takes on the donor phenotype; pioneered in Mycoplasma.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Project to build a redesigned S. cerevisiae genome maintaining wild-type phenotype while removing repeats/introns and adding thousands of loxP sites; ~6/16 chromosomes completed as of 2017.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Sequencing DNA directly from environmental samples to sample uncultivable microbial diversity; one such project yielded ~1 billion bp and 1.2 million new genes.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: The ordered series of events (G1, S, G2, M) by which a cell duplicates its contents and divides.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Non-dividing "quiescent" state outside the active cell cycle.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Cyclin-CDK complex that drives cells into M phase; identified via Xenopus cytoplasm transfer experiments.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Regulatory protein whose levels rise and fall in the cell cycle; binds and activates Cdks to confer substrate specificity. Discovered by Tim Hunt.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Protein kinase whose activity requires binding to a cyclin; phosphorylates many substrates to drive cell-cycle transitions.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: S. cerevisiae gene encoding the single cell-cycle Cdk (Cdk1); temperature-sensitive cdc28 mutants were key in yeast cell cycle studies.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: S. pombe gene encoding the single fission-yeast Cdk (Cdk1); ortholog of CDC28.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Cell Division Cycle mutants; Hartwell isolated 148 ts yeast mutants representing ~32 genes that arrest at specific cell-cycle stages.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Nobel laureates (2001) for discoveries of key regulators of the cell cycle: CDC mutants (Hartwell), cyclins (Hunt), CDKs (Nurse).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Budding yeast G1 cyclins; CLN1 and CLN2 were isolated as high-copy suppressors of cdc28 mutants.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Regulatory control point that halts cell-cycle progression until specific conditions (DNA integrity, spindle attachment) are met.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Kinase needed for firing DNA replication origins and for replication fork progression.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Putative DNA helicase component required for DNA replication initiation.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Reversible covalent addition of a phosphate group (usually by a kinase); alters protein conformation and activity.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Covalent attachment of ubiquitin that targets proteins (e.g., cyclins) for proteasomal degradation.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Regulated cellular suicide in which the cell shrinks, chromatin condenses, DNA fragments, and phagocytes clear the remnants; normal part of development.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Uncontrolled cell death from acute injury; cells swell and burst, releasing contents and triggering inflammation.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: "Cell-death abnormal" genes in C. elegans; classic screen identified ced-1, ced-3, ced-4, ced-9 controlling apoptosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: C. elegans caspase; activated by CED-4 upon apoptosis stimulus; loss-of-function blocks all 131 developmental cell deaths.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: C. elegans apoptosis activator; normally sequestered by CED-9 but released to activate CED-3.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: C. elegans inhibitor of cell death; mammalian ortholog is bcl-2. Loss-of-function causes ectopic cell death; gain-of-function blocks it.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mammalian ortholog of ced-9 (B-Cell Lymphoma 2); anti-apoptotic, overexpression contributes to tumor formation by blocking apoptosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Cysteine-Aspartate-specific Protease; proteolytic enzyme (30–50 kDa) that carries out apoptosis by cleaving substrates C-terminal to Asp.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Inactive precursor of an enzyme, activated by proteolytic cleavage (e.g., procaspases to caspases).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Sequence in which an initiator caspase cleaves and activates downstream executioner caspases, amplifying the apoptotic signal.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Protein-interaction modules used to recruit caspases and adapters in death-receptor apoptotic signaling.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Include other caspases, nuclear lamins, inhibitors of DNases, gelsolin (actin-severing), and Bcl-2 family members.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mutation that produces novel or enhanced activity (often dominant); e.g., original ced-9 mutant blocking apoptosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mutation that reduces or abolishes gene function; most mutations are loss-of-function and recessive.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Long-lasting, dormant alternative larval stage in C. elegans that can survive ≥6 months under harsh conditions.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Two-division process producing haploid gametes from a diploid cell; generates genetic diversity through independent assortment and crossing over.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Stage of meiosis I during which homologous chromosomes pair and crossing over (recombination) occurs.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Nuclear intermediate filament protein; mutations cause progeria; caspases cleave lamins during apoptosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Adipose-derived hormone regulating food intake and body weight; the ob mutant mouse (leptin deficient) is obese.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Natural mouse variants collected from the 1700s in Asia and formalized by the National Mouse Club (1895, Victorian England); foundation of modern laboratory mouse strains.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: African clawed frog used in embryological and cell-free experiments that identified MPF.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Streptococcus pneumoniae; used by Griffith and Avery et al. in classical transformation experiments (smooth vs rough strains).

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Human cell line derived from Henrietta Lacks; cell extracts used to produce synthetic poliovirus from in-vitro-synthesized RNA.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Pufferfish with a compact vertebrate genome (~38,000 genes in ~400 Mb); model for comparative genomics.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Malaria-vector mosquito whose ~14,000-gene genome was sequenced for public-health genomics.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Classic fancy mouse phenotype caused by adrenal gland failure.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mouse mutant characterized by adult-onset obesity.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Mouse mutant with inner ear defects causing head shaking and deafness; also a Drosophila K+ channel gene.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Enzyme in pyrimidine nucleotide synthesis inhibited by 6-azauracil.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Contrasting regulatory mechanisms: proteolysis is irreversible and often activates zymogens (e.g., caspases); phosphorylation is reversible and modulates activity.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: 1859 publication establishing evolution by natural selection; cornerstone alongside Mendel (1866) and Miescher (1869) for origins of genetics.

entries.subject: Biology — Genetics
entries.definition: Discoverer of "nuclein" (DNA) in 1869, laying groundwork for the molecular era of genetics.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: A multipotent stem cell in bone marrow that gives rise to all blood and immune cells. Rare (~1 in 50,000) and capable of self-renewal.
entries.key_detail: Four fates — self renewal, differentiation, emigration, programmed cell death.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: A progenitor derived from HSC that can still give rise to multiple blood lineages but has limited self-renewal.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Progenitor cell that gives rise to myeloid lineage cells: erythrocytes, megakaryocytes, granulocytes, and monocytes.
entries.sources: `B. Hematopoiesis.pptx`, `C. Cells of the Immune system.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Progenitor that gives rise to lymphoid cells (T, B, NK, some dendritic cells).
entries.related: CMP, T cell, B cell, NK cell

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: White blood cell; nucleated blood cells of the immune system.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Anucleate blood cell packed with hemoglobin that transports oxygen.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Large bone marrow cell with a lobed nucleus that produces platelets.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Small anucleate cell fragment derived from megakaryocytes that mediates blood clotting.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Myeloid leukocyte with multilobed nucleus and cytoplasmic granules; includes neutrophils, basophils, and eosinophils.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Most abundant granulocyte; a professional phagocyte that destroys bacteria via phagocytosis, reactive oxygen species, antimicrobial peptides, and NETs.
entries.key_detail: Short lifespan 6–8 hours; peaks first 6 h of inflammation; stains with both acid and basic dyes.
entries.sources: `C. Cells of the Immune system.pptx`, `N. Effector responses 2022.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Rare granulocyte that stains with basic dye (methylene blue) and secretes histamine to mediate inflammation; closely related to mast cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Granulocyte stained red by eosin that helps destroy parasites and modulates allergic inflammation; secretes lytic enzymes, perforin, neurotoxins.
entries.sources: `C. Cells of the Immune system.pptx`, `N. Effector responses 2022.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Tissue-resident granulocyte-like cell found in vascularized tissues; releases histamine, TNFα, and other mediators on degranulation; key initiator of inflammation and type I hypersensitivity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Circulating mononuclear phagocyte that migrates into tissues and matures into macrophages.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Large, long-lived tissue phagocyte derived from monocytes; kills microbes with lysosomal enzymes, superoxide, hypochlorite; also secretes complement.
entries.key_detail: Second only to liver in complement synthesis; professional APC.
entries.sources: `C. Cells of the Immune system.pptx`, `N. Effector responses 2022.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Sentinel antigen-presenting cell with long membranous extensions; the main driver of naive T-cell activation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Dendritic cell resident in the epidermis; involved in type IV (contact) hypersensitivity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Small mononuclear leukocyte of the adaptive immune system; includes B cells, T cells, and NK cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Lymphocyte that matures in bone marrow, expresses a surface B-cell receptor (membrane Ig), and produces antibodies upon activation.
entries.key_detail: Name derives from Bursa of Fabricius in birds.
entries.sources: `C. Cells of the Immune system.pptx`, `L. B cell maturation.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Terminally differentiated antibody-secreting B cell; can produce ~2000 antibody molecules per second.
entries.sources: `C. Cells of the Immune system.pptx`, `E. Antigens and Antibodies.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Lymphocyte that matures in the thymus and expresses a T-cell receptor; includes helper (CD4) and cytotoxic (CD8) subsets.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: T lymphocyte expressing CD4 that recognizes antigen on MHC II and orchestrates immune responses by secreting cytokines and activating B cells and macrophages.
entries.sources: `J. MHC in Antigen presentation.pptx`, `N. Effector responses 2022.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: CD8+ T lymphocyte that recognizes antigen on MHC I and kills virus-infected or tumor cells using perforin, granzymes, and Fas/FasL.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Helper T-cell subset that promotes cell-mediated immunity by activating cytotoxic T cells and macrophages; drives IgG class switching and delayed-type hypersensitivity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Helper T-cell subset that activates B cells to secrete antibodies (especially IgE); responds to extracellular pathogens and parasites.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Helper T-cell subset that secretes IL-17 and mediates defense against extracellular bacteria and fungi; involved in inflammation/autoimmunity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: T-cell subset that suppresses immune responses and maintains self-tolerance.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Innate lymphocyte that kills tumor cells and virus-infected cells, especially those lacking surface MHC I; uses perforin/granzymes like CTLs.
entries.sources: `C. Cells of the Immune system.pptx`, `N. Effector responses 2022.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: T cell subset that recognizes non-peptide (lipid) antigens presented by CD1.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Immature T cell developing in the thymus; >95% die without reaching maturity.
entries.sources: `C. Organs of the Immune System.pptx`, `K2. T-cell maturation.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: An activated, proliferating lymphocyte precursor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Connective tissue cell (fibroblast, pericyte) of an organ; in bone marrow and thymus supports hematopoiesis and lymphocyte maturation.
entries.sources: `C. Organs of the Immune System.pptx`, `K2. T-cell maturation.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Bone-forming cell that also helps regulate HSC proliferation in the bone marrow niche.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mature bone cell embedded in lacunae within bone matrix.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Principal cell type of the epidermis; secretes antimicrobial proteins such as psoriasin.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mucus-secreting epithelial cell lining internal surfaces (respiratory, GI tracts).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Specialized epithelial cell in Peyer's patches that samples antigen from the gut lumen and delivers it to underlying lymphoid tissue.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Nomenclature for cell-surface molecules identified by specific monoclonal antibodies; >300 CD markers defined.
entries.sources: `C. Cells of the Immune system.pptx`, `K2. T-cell maturation.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Invariant complex of 5 proteins (γδεζη forming three dimers) that associates with the TCR and transmits activation signals via ITAMs.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Co-receptor on helper T cells that binds conserved regions of MHC class II; also the HIV entry receptor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Co-receptor on cytotoxic T cells that binds conserved regions of MHC class I.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: B-cell surface marker used to isolate B cells (e.g., by FACS).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Complement receptor 2 on B cells; component of B-cell co-receptor that binds C3d-coated antigen.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Inhibitory co-receptor on B cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Costimulatory receptor on T cells that binds B7 (CD80/CD86) on APCs, providing Signal 2 for T-cell activation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Surface marker used to isolate hematopoietic stem cells via antibody-affinity columns.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: B-cell surface molecule that binds CD40L on activated helper T cells to deliver Signal 2 for B-cell activation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: T-cell ligand for CD40; delivers costimulatory signal to B cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Costimulatory ligands on antigen-presenting cells that bind CD28 (and CTLA-4) on T cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Inhibitory T-cell receptor that binds B7 with high affinity to dampen T-cell activation; target of checkpoint-blockade cancer immunotherapy.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: MHC-like molecule that presents non-peptide (lipid) antigens to NKT cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Site where lymphocytes develop and mature: bone marrow (B cells) and thymus (T cells).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Site where mature lymphocytes encounter antigen and are activated: lymph nodes, spleen, MALT.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Primary lymphoid organ within long bones; site of hematopoiesis and B-cell maturation. Red marrow is hematopoietic; yellow marrow is fatty.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
