entries.definition: Red marrow is active hematopoietic tissue; yellow marrow is fat-filled. At birth all marrow is red; ~50% converts to yellow.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Avian cloacal organ where B cells mature; origin of the "B" in B cell.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Primary lymphoid organ where T cells mature and undergo positive/negative selection. Weight declines from ~30 g to ~3 g with age.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cortex is the outer thymic region where positive selection occurs; medulla is the inner region where negative selection occurs.
entries.sources: `C. Organs of the Immune System.pptx`, `K2. T-cell maturation.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Concentric whorls of epithelial reticular cells in thymic medulla; source of cytokine TSLP that directs DC maturation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Thymic epithelial cell expressing AIRE-regulated tissue-specific proteins to enable negative selection of self-reactive T cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Transcription factor in mTECs that drives promiscuous expression of tissue-specific antigens for central tolerance.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Network of vessels and nodes that conveys lymph back to the bloodstream and integrates immune surveillance.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Encapsulated, bean-shaped secondary lymphoid organ where immune responses to lymph-borne antigens are mounted. Removes ~90% of antigen.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cortex is B-cell-rich (follicles); paracortex is T-cell-rich; medulla contains antibody-secreting plasma cells (medullary cords).
entries.sources: `C. Organs of the Immune System.pptx`, `L. B cell maturation.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cluster of naive B cells in lymphoid tissue before antigen encounter.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Follicle containing a germinal center where activated B cells undergo affinity maturation, class switching, and memory cell formation; surrounded by a mantle of small lymphocytes.
entries.sources: `C. Organs of the Immune System.pptx`, `L. B cell maturation.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Secondary lymphoid organ that filters blood, destroys old erythrocytes, and traps blood-borne antigens for presentation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Red pulp of spleen filters blood and removes old erythrocytes; white pulp contains lymphocytes (T cells and B cells).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Collective term for mucosal lymphoid tissue of gut, airways, and reproductive tract; includes GALT, BALT, tonsils, Peyer's patches, appendix.
entries.sources: `C. Organs of the Immune System.pptx`, `L. B cell maturation.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Lymphoid tissue of the digestive tract; includes Peyer's patches and the appendix.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mucosal lymphoid tissue in the airways.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Lymphoid follicles in the wall of the small intestine that trap GI antigens via M cells and permit B/T cell interaction.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: MALT at the pharynx; lymphoid tissue that traps airborne and ingested antigens.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Fluid in spaces between cells; 3 L/day enters lymphatics to become lymph.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Specialized post-capillary venule in lymph nodes where lymphocytes exit the bloodstream (extravasation).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Non-specific defense ready prior to pathogen exposure; consists of anatomical barriers, chemical defenses, and cellular responses.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antigen-specific immunity mediated by B and T cells; induced by exposure to antigens and generates memory.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Physical boundary (skin, mucous membranes) that prevents pathogen entry.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: 11.5 kDa antimicrobial protein secreted by keratinocytes in response to wounds; kills bacteria directly and activates neutrophils.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Large, heavily O-glycosylated secreted glycoproteins that form mucus; trap microbes at epithelial surfaces.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cilia-driven clearance of mucus-trapped particles from respiratory tract; part of innate defense.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Enzyme secreted by neutrophils, macrophages, and submucosal glands that digests peptidoglycan cell walls of Gram-positive bacteria.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cationic antimicrobial peptide made by granulocytes that binds negative charges on bacterial walls and forms pores. α-defensins are 29–35 aa; β-defensins up to 45 aa.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Structurally diverse antimicrobial peptide family with broad microbicidal activity; disrupts membranes and activates other cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Non-oxidative neutrophil protein that kills Gram-negative bacteria.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Conserved microbial molecular feature (e.g., LPS, flagellin, dsRNA) recognized by innate receptors.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Host receptor that recognizes PAMPs to trigger innate responses (e.g., TLRs, NLRs).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Membrane-bound PRR family that detects microbial PAMPs and initiates signal transduction leading to cytokine production. Named after Drosophila Toll.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Toll-like receptor that recognizes peptidoglycan of Gram-positive bacteria.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Toll-like receptor that recognizes LPS (endotoxin) of Gram-negative bacteria.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cytoplasmic pattern recognition receptor family that detects intracellular PAMPs and danger signals.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Outer-membrane component of Gram-negative bacteria; potent PAMP detected by TLR-4 that drives inflammation and can cause septic shock.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cross-linked polysaccharide-peptide cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria; PAMP sensed by TLR-2.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Engulfment of particles (microbes, dead cells) by professional phagocytes, forming a phagosome that fuses with lysosomes.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cells specialized for phagocytosis: neutrophils, macrophages, dendritic cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Discharge of contents of cytoplasmic granules by activated cells (neutrophils, mast cells, basophils, eosinophils).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Toxic oxygen-derived products (superoxide O2−, hypochlorite HOCl) generated by phagocytes to kill microbes.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Web of expelled chromatin coated with antimicrobial proteins that neutrophils release to trap and kill pathogens.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: First cytokine discovered; family of antiviral proteins that block translation, promote RNA degradation, and enhance CTL responses.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antiviral cytokines produced by virally infected cells that induce an antiviral state in neighboring cells; induce PKR.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pro-inflammatory cytokine secreted by Th1 cells and NK cells; activates macrophages and promotes MHC expression.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Interferon-induced dsRNA-activated kinase that phosphorylates eIF2α to block translation in infected cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Complex innate response characterized by swelling (tumor), redness (rubor), heat (calor), and pain (dolor); driven by IL-1, TNFα, IL-6.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Accumulation of interstitial fluid causing swelling during inflammation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Movement of leukocytes from blood vessels through the endothelium into surrounding tissue during inflammation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Elevated body temperature triggered by IL-1 and TNFα acting on hypothalamus; prostaglandin-mediated resetting of thermostat.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Host cytokine (IL-1, TNFα, IL-6) that induces fever via the hypothalamic OVLT and prostaglandins.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Microbial substance (PAMP) that induces fever by triggering innate cytokine release.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Vasoactive amine released by mast cells and basophils; increases capillary permeability in inflammation and allergy.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Lipid mediator released from the OVLT that raises hypothalamic temperature set point; target of NSAIDs.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Drug class that inhibits cyclooxygenase and prostaglandin synthesis to reduce inflammation, pain, and fever.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Non-sedating H1-histamine receptor antagonist used to treat allergies.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Localized swelling and reddening from mast-cell histamine release during type I hypersensitivity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: ~30 serum and membrane proteins of innate and adaptive immunity that lyse pathogens, promote phagocytosis, and amplify inflammation.
entries.key_detail: Discovered 1894 by Bordet; named by Ehrlich.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Complement activation initiated by antibody (IgM or IgG) binding antigen, engaging C1qrs.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Complement activation initiated on foreign cell surfaces by spontaneous C3 hydrolysis stabilized by microbial surfaces; factor B, properdin involved.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Complement activation initiated when mannose-binding lectin (MBL) binds mannose/fucose residues on microbial surfaces; activates MASP.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: First complement component of the classical pathway: C1q (recognition) with two C1r and two C1s serine proteases.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Serum lectin that recognizes mannose/fucose on microbial surfaces and initiates the lectin complement pathway.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Serine protease that cleaves C4 and C2 after MBL binds a microbe, initiating the lectin pathway.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Central complement protein (185 kDa); cleavage to C3a and C3b is the convergence point of all three pathways.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Large fragment of C3 that opsonizes microbes, binds membranes, and forms C5 convertase.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Small complement cleavage fragments that promote inflammation and mast-cell degranulation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Enzyme complex (C4b2a classical/lectin; C3bBb alternative) that cleaves C3 into C3a and C3b.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Enzyme complex (C4b2a3b or C3bBb3b) that cleaves C5 to initiate MAC assembly.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Large C5 fragment that binds target cell surface and nucleates MAC assembly; stabilized by C6.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pore-forming complex (C5b-C6-C7-C8 plus 10–17 C9) that lyses target cells; makes a 70–100 Å hole.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Coating of a pathogen (often by C3b or antibody) to enhance phagocytosis. Literally "to make tasty."

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Serum protein that stabilizes C3bBb convertase of the alternative pathway.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cleavage product of C3b that binds to antigens and engages CR2/CD21 on B cells as a costimulatory signal.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Any molecule that can provoke or be recognized by an adaptive immune response.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Specific region of an antigen recognized by an antibody or T-cell receptor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Small molecule that elicits an immune response only when attached to a large carrier protein.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Substance that enhances the immune response to an antigen without being antigenic itself (e.g., Freund's complete adjuvant).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antigen emulsified in mineral oil with heat-killed mycobacteria; strong immunopotentiator used in research.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gamma-globulin protein produced by B cells/plasma cells that binds a specific antigen; ~20% of total blood protein.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: 50 kDa antibody subunit (two per molecule); its constant region defines isotype (μ, δ, γ, ε, α).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: 22 kDa antibody subunit (two per molecule); exists as kappa (κ) or lambda (λ) types.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: One of two antibody light chain types; encoded at 2p11 with 40 V, 5 J, 1 C segments.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: One of two antibody light chain types; 30 V, 4 J, 4 C segments.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Amino-terminal portion of antibody (or TCR) chain that differs between clones and contains the antigen-binding site.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Carboxyl-terminal portion of antibody that determines effector functions and isotype.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: "Fragment antigen-binding" piece of an antibody produced by papain digestion; contains the antigen-binding site.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: "Fragment crystallizable" portion of an antibody (lower heavy-chain constant region); binds Fc receptors and activates complement.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cysteine protease that cleaves antibodies into two Fab fragments and one Fc fragment.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Compact domain of two β-sheets linked by a disulfide bond; structural building block of Igs, TCRs, MHC, and the Ig superfamily.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Large family of proteins sharing the Ig fold, including antibodies, TCRs, MHC, CD4, CD8, and many adhesion molecules.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Short, highly variable sequences within antibody V regions that form antigen-contact loops.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Three hypervariable loops per chain (CDR1, CDR2, CDR3) that together form the antigen-binding site of antibodies and TCRs.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody class defined by heavy-chain constant region (IgM, IgD, IgG, IgE, IgA).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Set of antigenic determinants within the variable region of a given antibody; unique to that clone.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: First antibody class on developing B cells and first secreted in primary responses; secreted as a pentamer with a J chain; excellent at agglutination and complement activation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Most abundant serum antibody (~80%); dominates secondary responses; four subclasses (IgG1–4); only isotype that crosses the placenta.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Predominant secreted antibody (saliva, tears, milk, respiratory/intestinal secretions); dimeric with J chain; transcytosed across epithelia. Secreted in quantities greater than all other antibodies combined.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody class that binds Fc receptors on mast cells and basophils to trigger histamine release; mediates type I hypersensitivity and parasite defense.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody class expressed with IgM on naive B cells; no known major biological effector function.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Small (159 aa) polypeptide that joins monomers of polymeric IgM (pentamer) and IgA (dimer).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cell-surface receptor that binds the Fc region of antibodies, enabling opsonized phagocytosis, ADCC, and mast-cell activation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Refers to antibodies' ability to bind two identical antigen epitopes via two Fab arms.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Movement of polymeric antibodies (IgA, IgM) across epithelial layers via the poly-Ig receptor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Epithelial receptor that transcytoses IgA/IgM across mucosal surfaces and contributes the secretory component.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Liquid portion of blood containing clotting factors; cells removed.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Plasma without clotting factors; obtained after blood has clotted.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Serum protein fraction rich in antibodies, separated by electrophoresis.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cross-linking of cells/particles by antibodies (especially IgM) into clumps.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Site-specific somatic DNA recombination that assembles functional antibody (and TCR) genes from V, D, and J gene segments during lymphocyte development.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gene segments encoding most of the variable domain of an antibody/TCR chain.
entries.key_detail: Heavy chain has 65 V; κ has 40; λ has 30.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gene segment found only in heavy chains and TCR β/δ; joins V to J; 27 D segments in human heavy chain locus.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Short gene segment joining V (or D) to the constant region.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gene locus containing 65 V, 27 D, 6 J, and 9 C segments; rearranges D-to-J first, then V-to-DJ.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Conserved heptamer-spacer-nonamer DNA signals flanking V, D, J segments that direct recombination.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Recombination only occurs between RSSs with 12-bp and 23-bp spacers, ensuring correct segment joining.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Lymphocyte-specific endonucleases that induce double-strand breaks at RSSs during V(D)J recombination; both required.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Nucleotide loss or addition (N- and P-nucleotides) at V(D)J junctions that expands receptor diversity.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Palindromic nucleotides added at V(D)J junctions due to hairpin-opening during recombination.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Non-templated nucleotides (up to 15) added by TdT at V(D)J junctions.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Template-independent polymerase expressed in developing lymphocytes that adds N-nucleotides during V(D)J joining.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mechanism ensuring that each B (or T) cell expresses antibody/TCR from only one allele of each chain, yielding monospecificity.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: 10^3–10^6 fold increase in point mutations in rearranged Ig V regions in germinal centers; drives affinity maturation.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Progressive increase in average antibody affinity during an immune response, driven by SHM and selection in germinal centers.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: DNA recombination at heavy-chain switch regions that changes antibody isotype (e.g., IgM → IgG) while preserving V region specificity.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Enzyme that deaminates cytosine to uracil in Ig genes; required for both class-switch recombination and somatic hypermutation.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cancerous plasma cell that continuously divides and secretes a single antibody; fused with B cells to make hybridomas.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`, `Q. Monoclonal Antibodies.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: B-cell lymphoma caused by translocation placing the c-myc oncogene next to the Ig heavy-chain enhancer due to improper recombination.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: cis-acting DNA element brought near Ig promoters by V(D)J recombination that greatly increases transcription.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gene complex encoding cell-surface molecules required for antigen presentation to T cells and rapid graft rejection; called H-2 in mouse and HLA in humans.
entries.sources: `J. MHC in Antigen presentation.pptx`, `K. MHC Gene organizatin.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Human MHC gene complex; includes HLA-A, -B, -C (class I) and HLA-DP, -DQ, -DR (class II).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mouse MHC complex.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Glycoproteins expressed on nearly all nucleated cells; present 8–10 aa intracellular peptides to CD8+ cytotoxic T cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Glycoproteins expressed primarily on professional APCs (macrophages, DCs, B cells); present 13–18 aa extracellular peptides to CD4+ helper T cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: MHC-linked genes encoding immune components such as complement proteins and inflammation-related molecules.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: The three classical class I HLA loci with thousands of alleles (HLA-A >2000, HLA-B >2600, HLA-C >1500).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Classical class II HLA loci expressed on professional APCs.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Each individual expresses multiple different MHC genes: up to 6 class I and 12+ class II molecules.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: MHC loci have many (thousands) of alleles in the population, maximizing peptide-binding diversity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Set of MHC alleles of linked genes present on one parental chromosome; inherited as a block.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Both maternal and paternal MHC alleles are simultaneously expressed on each cell.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Small invariant light chain of class I MHC; required for cell-surface expression.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Multi-subunit cytosolic protease complex that degrades ubiquitin-tagged proteins into peptides for class I MHC loading.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Small protein tag that marks substrates for proteasomal degradation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: ER membrane pump that transports proteasome-derived peptides into the ER for loading onto class I MHC.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Chaperone that blocks the peptide-binding groove of class II MHC in the ER and directs it to endosomes.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Residual peptide of the invariant chain that occupies the class II groove until exchanged for an antigenic peptide by HLA-DM.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cell that displays antigenic peptide on MHC II to T cells; professional APCs are dendritic cells, macrophages, and B cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Loading of exogenously acquired antigens onto MHC class I by certain dendritic cells, permitting CD8 T-cell activation against non-infected sources.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Internalization of extracellular material by invagination of the plasma membrane.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Uptake of specific ligands bound to cell-surface receptors, producing coated vesicles.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Property that a given T-cell clone recognizes peptide only in the context of specific self-MHC molecules; demonstrated by Zinkernagel and Doherty.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Heterodimeric antigen receptor on T cells that recognizes peptide in the context of MHC; monovalent, Ig-superfamily member.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Predominant T-cell receptor type (90–99%); recognizes peptide-MHC.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Minor T-cell receptor type; recognizes non-peptide/species-specific antigens without classical MHC presentation; behaves more like an innate pattern receptor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: One of two chains of the αβ TCR, analogous to antibody light chain (VJ rearrangement only).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: One of two chains of the αβ TCR, analogous to heavy chain (VDJ rearrangement); rearranges first.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Conserved cytoplasmic sequence motif (in CD3, Igα/Igβ) that becomes tyrosine-phosphorylated upon receptor engagement to recruit signaling kinases.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Src-family tyrosine kinase associated with CD4/CD8 that phosphorylates CD3 ITAMs to initiate TCR signaling.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Tyrosine kinase recruited to phosphorylated CD3 ζ-chain ITAMs; key downstream mediator of TCR signaling.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Engineered T-cell receptor combining an antibody-derived scFv with TCR signaling domains; used in CAR-T cancer therapy.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Viral or bacterial protein that binds simultaneously to TCR Vβ and MHC class II α chain outside the peptide groove, polyclonally activating many T cells (toxic shock, food poisoning).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Signaling receptor required for T-cell fate commitment; driving Notch in bone marrow yields T cells, disabling it in thymus yields B cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Early thymocyte that expresses neither CD4 nor CD8.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cortical thymocyte that expresses both CD4 and CD8 and undergoes positive and negative selection; ~98% die in thymus.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mature thymocyte expressing either CD4 or CD8 that exits the thymus as a naive T cell.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Thymic process selecting DP thymocytes whose TCRs weakly recognize self-MHC for survival; failure results in "death by neglect."

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Elimination of thymocytes whose TCRs bind self-MHC/self-peptide too strongly; enforces central self-tolerance.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Apoptosis of DP thymocytes that fail positive selection because their TCRs do not recognize self-MHC.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: State in which the immune system does not react against self-antigens; enforced centrally (thymus/bone marrow) and peripherally.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Combined positive and negative selection that produces a T-cell repertoire that is self-MHC restricted and self-tolerant.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mature T cell that has exited the thymus but has not yet encountered antigen.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Earliest B-lineage cell in bone marrow; undergoes heavy-chain D-J rearrangement.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: B-cell stage after productive heavy-chain rearrangement; expresses pre-BCR.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: B cell after successful light-chain rearrangement expressing surface IgM; subject to central tolerance.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Bone-marrow-exiting B cell expressing both IgM and IgD that has not yet encountered antigen.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Membrane-bound immunoglobulin (mIg) on B cells, associated with Igα/Igβ ITAM-bearing signaling chains.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: ITAM-containing transmembrane proteins that pair with mIg to form the signaling-competent B-cell receptor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Elimination or editing of self-reactive B cells in bone marrow; ~90% of daily-produced B cells are culled.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mechanisms (anergy, Tregs, deletion) preventing mature lymphocyte responses to self in the periphery.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Secondary light-chain rearrangement that rescues self-reactive immature B cells by replacing a self-reactive receptor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antigen (usually protein) that requires helper T-cell assistance for B-cell activation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antigen (e.g., repetitive polysaccharide) that activates B cells without T-cell help.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: TCR-CD3 engagement by peptide-MHC with CD4 or CD8 coreceptor binding MHC II or I.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: CD28 on T cell binding B7 (CD80/CD86) on an APC; required for full activation and IL-2 production.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cytokine signals (notably IL-2) that drive clonal expansion after Signals 1 and 2.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cross-linking of membrane Ig by antigen, with B-cell coreceptor CR2/CD21 binding C3d-coated antigen.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: CD40 on B cell binding CD40L on activated helper T cell.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Second signal beyond antigen receptor required for full lymphocyte activation; absence leads to anergy.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: State of lymphocyte unresponsiveness induced when the antigen receptor is engaged without a second signal.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Rapid proliferation of antigen-specific lymphocytes upon activation; central event of adaptive responses.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Organized contact zone between a T cell and an APC where TCR, coreceptors, and adhesion molecules cluster.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Costimulatory ligands on APCs that engage CD28 or CTLA-4 on T cells.

entries.term: Helper T Cell Help (to B cell)
entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Interaction in which a Th cell recognizes peptide-MHC II on a B cell and delivers CD40L and cytokine signals to drive class switching and affinity maturation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mature T or B cell that has not yet encountered antigen.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Small (<30 kDa) secreted protein that signals between immune cells; exhibits pleiotropy, redundancy, synergy, antagonism, and cascade induction.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cytokine secreted by leukocytes (and others) that controls growth and differentiation of hematopoietic and immune cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Outdated term for cytokines secreted by lymphocytes (especially Th cells).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Outdated term for cytokines secreted by monocytes/macrophages.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Modes of cytokine action: on the secreting cell itself, nearby cells, or distant cells via bloodstream.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Ability of one cytokine to act on different cell types with different effects.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Multiple cytokines producing similar biological effects.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Two cytokines producing greater (synergy) or opposing (antagonism) effects than either alone.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: One cytokine triggering secretion of additional cytokines.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pro-inflammatory cytokine and endogenous pyrogen produced by macrophages, monocytes, neutrophils, and others in response to PAMPs.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: T-cell growth factor (TCGF) produced by activated Th cells; drives proliferation of T, B, and NK cells; 15 kDa.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Heterotrimeric receptor (α, β, γ chains) on activated lymphocytes; γ chain shared among several interleukin receptors.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cytokine receptor subunit shared by IL-2, IL-4, IL-7, IL-9, IL-15, IL-21 receptors; mutated in X-linked SCID.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Th2 cytokine driving IgE class switching and B-cell proliferation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pro-inflammatory cytokine produced by many cells; key driver of fever and COVID-19 cytokine storm.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Chemokine synthesized by endothelial cells and macrophages; neutrophil chemoattractant at low doses and degranulator at high doses.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pro-inflammatory mediator.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pro-inflammatory cytokine that drives Th1 differentiation and NK activation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cytokine functionally similar to IL-2; supports NK and memory T-cell survival.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pro-inflammatory cytokine secreted by Th17 cells; recruits neutrophils.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cytokine that regulates lymphocyte differentiation; shares common γ receptor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pro-inflammatory cytokine from macrophages, mast cells, activated T cells, NK cells and fibroblasts; induces apoptosis, fever, wasting, and acts as neutrophil chemoattractant.
entries.key_detail: Targeted by Enbrel (soluble receptor), Remicade, Humira for RA.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pro-inflammatory mediator and key regulator of Treg induction and tissue remodeling.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Hematopoietin-family cytokine that drives myeloid differentiation; pro-inflammatory.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cytokine that mobilizes peripheral-blood stem cells; used prior to HSC harvest.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Low molecular weight chemotactic cytokine that directs leukocyte migration and regulates integrin expression.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Chemokines secreted by endothelial cells that recruit monocytes to sites of inflammation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Chemokine ("Regulated upon Activation, Normal T cell Expressed and Secreted") that attracts T cells, eosinophils, basophils.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Chemokine that selectively recruits eosinophils.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Phospholipid mediator that activates platelets and recruits leukocytes.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Chemokine receptor on activated T cells, monocytes, macrophages; co-receptor for HIV entry.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pathological release of extremely high cytokine levels (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6) leading to systemic damage; cause of sepsis and severe COVID-19.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Signaling cascade used by many cytokine receptors: Janus kinases phosphorylate STAT transcription factors, which dimerize and translocate to the nucleus.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cytokine receptor superfamily used by IL-2, IL-4, GM-CSF and many others; signals via JAK-STAT.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Family of transcription factors that regulate type I interferon responses.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Soluble TNF-α receptor fusion protein used to treat rheumatoid arthritis.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Monoclonal antibodies against TNF-α used in RA and other inflammatory diseases.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody-mediated immunity effective against extracellular pathogens and toxins.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: T-cell-driven immunity against intracellular pathogens, viruses, and tumors; responsible for graft rejection.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Downstream activities of antibodies including neutralization, opsonization, agglutination, complement activation, and ADCC.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pore-forming protein stored in CTL and NK granules that punches holes in target cell membranes.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Serine protease(s) in CTL/NK granules that enter target cells through perforin pores and trigger apoptosis.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Death receptor-ligand pair used by CTLs to induce apoptosis in target cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Programmed, non-inflammatory cell death characterized by DNA fragmentation and membrane blebbing.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Uncontrolled, inflammatory cell death following injury.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Killing of antibody-coated target cells by NK cells (or others) that bind Fc with FcγRIII (CD16).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Combined use of phagocytosis, reactive oxygen species, antimicrobial proteins, and NETs to destroy microbes.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Development of naive CD8+ T cells into cytotoxic T lymphocytes following dendritic-cell antigen presentation and cytokine signals.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Immune response on first exposure to an antigen; slow onset, low magnitude, dominated by IgM.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Faster, stronger, longer-lasting response to re-encounter with antigen; dominated by IgG; requires far less antigen.
entries.sources: `L. B cell maturation.pptx`, `Antibodies and Diagnostic Testing.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Long-lived B cell generated in germinal centers that can rapidly respond to re-exposure; some persist for life.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antigen-experienced T cell that persists after infection clearance and responds rapidly on re-encounter; includes SCM, CM, EM, RM subsets.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Memory T cell subset that recirculates through lymphoid tissue and has high proliferative potential.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Memory T cell that patrols peripheral tissues and rapidly acquires effector function.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Memory T cell that remains in peripheral tissue (e.g., skin, lung) and provides rapid local defense.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Least differentiated memory T subset with high self-renewal capacity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Apoptotic elimination of ≥90% of effector lymphocytes after pathogen clearance, leaving a memory pool.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Preparation of immunogenic material used to safely induce protective adaptive immunity (memory cells) against a pathogen.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Adaptive immunity generated by natural infection or vaccination; produces memory.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Short-term protection from transferred preformed antibodies (e.g., maternal IgG, rabies immune globulin, therapeutic monoclonals).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pre-vaccination practice of deliberately exposing individuals to smallpox scabs/pustules to induce immunity (China/India, 11th century).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Weakened live pathogen that retains immunogenicity but not virulence; long-lasting immunity (e.g., MMR, OPV, chickenpox, FluMist).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Whole pathogen killed by heat or chemicals; safe but usually requires boosters (e.g., IPV, hepatitis A, rabies, whole-cell pertussis).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Purified protein, polysaccharide, or peptide antigen; highly safe (e.g., hepatitis B, acellular pertussis).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Chemically inactivated bacterial toxin that no longer causes disease but still induces neutralizing antibodies; ~10-year protection (e.g., tetanus, diphtheria).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Polysaccharide antigen linked to a carrier protein to recruit T-cell help (e.g., Hib, meningococcus, pneumococcus).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Vaccine using non-infectious particles that resemble viruses (e.g., HPV).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Vaccine using Gram-negative bacterial outer-membrane vesicles carrying antigens.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Plasmid DNA or viral vector encoding pathogen antigen; host cells produce antigen in situ.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Vaccine delivering modified mRNA in lipid nanoparticles that instructs host cells to produce pathogen antigen (e.g., Pfizer/BioNTech and Moderna COVID-19 vaccines).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Lipid delivery vehicle (ionizable lipid, phospholipid, cholesterol, PEGylated lipid) that protects and delivers modified mRNA vaccines.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Process of weakening a pathogen (e.g., cold-adapted growth for FluMist).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Poxvirus used as the smallpox vaccine due to cross-protective immunity against variola.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: 1955 polio vaccine batch in which incomplete formalin inactivation caused polio cases; a cautionary tale in vaccine manufacturing.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Formalin-inactivated polio vaccine developed by Jonas Salk (1955).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Live attenuated polio vaccine developed by Albert Sabin (1958).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Combined live attenuated measles, mumps, and rubella vaccine.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Indirect protection afforded to non-immune individuals when a high proportion of a population is immune, blocking pathogen transmission.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Only human infectious disease eradicated from nature; certified by WHO in 1979.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: English physician who developed the first smallpox vaccine using cowpox in 1794 ("vacca" = cow).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Mixture of antibodies from many B-cell clones that recognizes multiple epitopes on the same antigen; generated by serum immunization.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody from a single B-cell clone recognizing one epitope; produced from hybridomas for uniform reagents or therapeutics.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Immortal antibody-producing cell line formed by fusing a B cell with a myeloma cell using PEG; selected in HAT medium.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Reagent that promotes cell-cell fusion for hybridoma production.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Selection medium (Hypoxanthine, Aminopterin, Thymidine) that kills unfused HGPRT- myeloma cells, permitting only hybridomas (with HGPRT from B cell) to survive.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase; purine salvage enzyme lacking in myeloma partners used for HAT selection.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Engineered antibody with mouse variable regions and human constant regions to reduce immunogenicity; "-ximab" suffix.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody in which only mouse CDR loops are grafted onto a human framework; "-zumab" suffix.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody produced entirely from human sequences (from humanized mice like VelocImmune or convalescent B cells); "-umab" suffix.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Therapeutic mAb naming convention indicating origin: -omab (mouse), -ximab (chimeric), -zumab (humanized), -umab (fully human).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Transgenic mouse with human antibody gene loci used to generate fully human monoclonal antibodies.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Hairless mouse strain lacking a thymus (thus athymic/T-cell deficient); used as a permissive host for human tissues.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Regeneron monoclonal antibodies that bind the SARS-CoV-2 spike and block its binding to ACE2 (passive COVID immunity cocktail).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Combination of monoclonal antibodies binding different epitopes to improve efficacy and resist escape mutations.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Exaggerated or inappropriate immune response causing tissue damage; classified I–IV by Gell and Coombs.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: IgE-mediated, mast-cell-driven immediate allergic reaction (hay fever, asthma, anaphylaxis).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody-mediated (IgG/IgM) cytotoxicity directed at cell-surface antigens (e.g., hemolytic transfusion reaction).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Immune-complex-mediated disease (antigen-antibody complexes deposit in tissues), e.g., serum sickness, lupus.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: T-cell-mediated reaction occurring 48–72 h after antigen exposure; no antibodies (e.g., poison ivy, TB skin test).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Scheme classifying hypersensitivity reactions into types I–IV based on mechanism.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Seasonal IgE-mediated allergy to pollen (e.g., ragweed pectate lyase "antigen E").

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Major ragweed (Ambrosia) pollen allergen responsible for hay fever; source of "E" in IgE name.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Oily antigen in poison ivy that triggers Type IV contact hypersensitivity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Type IV hypersensitivity skin reaction following sensitization to haptens (e.g., urushiol, oxazolone).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Controlled, escalating allergen exposure shifting response toward Th1/IgG and away from IgE; prevents mast-cell degranulation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Theory that insufficient early-childhood microbial exposure increases susceptibility to allergies and autoimmunity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Life-threatening systemic Type I hypersensitivity with widespread mast-cell degranulation; treated with epinephrine.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Pathological immune response against self-antigens due to breakdown of self-tolerance (escape of self-reactive lymphocytes from negative selection).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Disease in which the immune system targets a particular tissue (e.g., thyroid in Graves', β-cells in T1D, skin in vitiligo).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Autoimmune disease affecting many organs (e.g., SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, scleroderma, dermatomyositis).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Autoimmune destruction of insulin-producing pancreatic β-cells in the islets of Langerhans by cytotoxic T cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Systemic autoimmune disease with autoantibodies to DNA, nuclear proteins, RBC, and platelet membranes; causes fever, rash, arthritis, kidney dysfunction.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Autoimmune demyelinating disease of the CNS.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Systemic autoimmune disease with chronic joint inflammation; treated with anti-TNFα biologics.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Inbred mouse strain that spontaneously develops a T1D-like autoimmune diabetes; islet CTL infiltration.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Endocrine cluster in the pancreas; β-cells produce insulin; target of T1D autoimmunity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Insulin-producing endocrine cell in pancreatic islets; destroyed in type 1 diabetes.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody directed against self-antigens; found in many autoimmune diseases.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Retrovirus that infects CD4+ cells (helper T cells, macrophages, dendritic cells); progressive T-cell depletion causes AIDS.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Two HIV types; HIV-1 (from SIVcpz, ~1920s, 90% of infections) and HIV-2 (from SIVsmm, sooty mangabey, ~1945).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: End-stage HIV infection characterized by severe CD4 T-cell depletion and opportunistic infections/cancers.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV envelope surface glycoprotein that binds CD4 on target cells; cleaved from gp160 by furin.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV transmembrane envelope protein that mediates fusion with the host membrane; remains associated with gp120.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV envelope polyprotein precursor cleaved by furin in the Golgi into gp120 (SU) and gp41 (TM).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Chemokine receptor that serves as a co-receptor for HIV entry alongside CD4; individuals with CCR5Δ32 mutation are resistant.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Alternate HIV co-receptor used by later-stage viral variants.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV enzyme that copies viral RNA genome into DNA; target of nucleoside analog drugs like AZT.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV enzyme that inserts proviral DNA into host chromosomes.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV enzyme that cleaves Gag and Pol polyproteins during maturation; target of protease inhibitor drugs.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV structural precursor cleaved into matrix (MA), capsid (CA), nucleocapsid (NC), and p6 proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV precursor cleaved to produce protease (PR), reverse transcriptase (RT), and integrase (IN).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV gene encoding envelope polyprotein gp160 (cleaved to gp120 and gp41).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Repeated sequence flanking the integrated HIV provirus; contains promoters and enhancers.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Nucleoside reverse-transcriptase inhibitor; first approved anti-HIV drug.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Combination antiretroviral therapy using RT, protease, integrase, and/or entry inhibitors to suppress HIV replication and prevent resistance.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: HIV transfer where dendritic cells carry virus from mucosal surfaces to CD4+ T cells in lymph nodes without being productively infected.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: First person cured of HIV via stem cell transplant from a CCR5Δ32/Δ32 donor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Congenital (usually genetic) defect in immune function.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Acquired immune defect (e.g., HIV, malnutrition, irradiation, chemotherapy, corticosteroids, aging, splenectomy, leukemias).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Genetic disease in which adaptive immunity fails due to absent/defective T cells (and often B and NK cells); fatal without treatment.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: SCID caused by adenosine deaminase deficiency; accumulation of deoxyadenosine/dATP kills immature T and B cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Enzyme that converts adenosine to inosine; deficiency causes ADA-SCID.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: SCID due to mutations in the common γ chain (IL-2Rγ) on the X chromosome, affecting receptors for IL-2, -4, -7, -9, -15, -21.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Insertion of a functional gene into a patient's cells to correct a genetic defect; used to treat ADA-SCID and SCID-X1 using retroviral vectors.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gene therapy performed on germ cells; heritable; not currently recommended for humans.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gene therapy on non-germline cells; not heritable.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Murine leukemia virus vector used to deliver therapeutic genes into patient cells in early gene-therapy trials.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Guide-RNA-directed nuclease system used for precise genome editing; allows targeted correction and reduced off-target insertions in gene therapy.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Treatment using hematopoietic stem cells (from cord blood, bone marrow, or mobilized peripheral blood) to reconstitute the immune system in leukemia, SCID, etc.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Transplant from a different HLA-matched donor.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Self-transplant using the patient's own cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Technique that size-fractionates proteins by SDS-PAGE, transfers them to membrane, and detects with specific antibody and labeled secondary antibody.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Blotting technique for DNA (gel-transfer-probe hybridization).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Blotting technique for RNA.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Anionic detergent that denatures and coats proteins with uniform negative charge for size-based electrophoresis; solubilizes membranes.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Reducing agents that break disulfide bonds in proteins prior to SDS-PAGE.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gel matrix used to size-fractionate proteins (or nucleic acids) by electrophoresis.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody that binds the antigen of interest in an immunoassay.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Labeled antibody (e.g., goat anti-rabbit-HRP) that binds the primary antibody to amplify and detect signal.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Enzyme conjugated to secondary antibodies; catalyzes luminol oxidation producing chemiluminescence for detection.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Enzyme conjugate on secondary antibodies that hydrolyzes substrates to colored or fluorescent products.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Chemiluminescent substrate oxidized by HRP to emit light detected on X-ray film or CCD.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Visualization of antigens in cells/tissues using fluorophore-conjugated antibodies.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gold-conjugated antibody used for antigen localization in electron microscopy.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Isolation of an antigen from lysate by binding to antibody coupled to beads.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: IP used to pull down protein complexes and identify interacting proteins.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Plate-based assay for quantifying antigens or antibodies using capture antibodies, sample, detection antibody, and enzyme-substrate color change.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: ELISA variant that detects cytokine secretion from individual cells as spots on a membrane.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Technique that analyzes cells one at a time in a fluid stream by their light scatter and fluorescence properties.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Flow cytometry variant that physically separates cells based on fluorescent antibody labels (e.g., sorting CD19+ B cells).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Enrichment technique that isolates cells (e.g., HSCs) by binding to antibody-coated surfaces.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody binding to antigens other than the intended target; a common complication of immunoassays.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Instruments for freezing and sectioning tissues (e.g., 10 μm sections) for histology and immunofluorescence.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Dye used to stain total protein in polyacrylamide gels.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Fluorescent DNA-binding dye used to stain nuclei in immunofluorescence.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Test that uses antibody-antigen binding to detect either an antigen (antigen-detecting) or an antibody (antibody-detecting) in a sample.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Test that uses antibody reagents to detect the presence of a pathogen antigen (e.g., HBsAg, SARS-CoV-2 rapid test).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Test for patient-serum antibodies against a pathogen (indicates past infection or vaccination).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Analysis of antibody responses in serum for infection diagnosis or immune-status testing.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Automated fluorescence immunoassay system using solid-phase receptacles; measures fluorescence at 450 nm from enzyme-substrate product.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Most reliable marker of active HBV infection; detectable weeks before liver damage appears.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Antibody against HBV surface antigen; appears ~6 months after acute infection and indicates recovery/immunity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: IgM antibody against the HBV core antigen; appears early in infection and marks recent infection.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Best indication of active HBV viral replication; quantitation predicts treatment response.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Partially dsDNA Hepadnavirus causing liver disease; transmitted through body fluids; 1–3 month incubation; 400 million cases worldwide.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Non-treponemal syphilis screening test detecting antibodies to cardiolipin/lecithin released by infected cells; not specific for T. pallidum.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Spiral bacterium causing syphilis; cannot be cultured; diagnosed by serology, dark-field microscopy, fluorescent antibody, or PCR.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Multiplexed fluorescence immunoassay platform used in clinical antibody panel testing.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Additional vaccine dose given to re-stimulate memory response and restore waning immunity.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Immunoassay result that is borderline; patient should be retested or given booster.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Development of detectable antibodies after infection or vaccination; result changes from non-reactive to reactive.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Amplification of specific DNA sequences; used in diagnostics to detect pathogens like HBV and T. pallidum.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Microscopy technique used to visualize live spirochetes (e.g., T. pallidum) against a dark background.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Preparation of anti-HBV antibodies providing passive post-exposure protection.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Concentrated anti-rabies antibodies (150 IU/mL) from hyperimmunized human plasma; given for rapid post-exposure protection alongside vaccine.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: IgG transferred across the placenta (or IgA via milk) that protects infants for months but can block live vaccine responses.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Unspecialized cell capable of self-renewal and differentiation into specialized cell types.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Stem cell that can give rise to any cell type of the body plus extraembryonic tissues (e.g., zygote, ICM cells).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Stem cell that can give rise to any fetal or adult cell type but not extraembryonic tissue.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Stem cell that can give rise to a limited number of cell lineages (e.g., HSC).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Stem cell that differentiates into only one cell type.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Totipotent cell derived from the inner cell mass of a blastocyst; can give rise to all body cell types.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Adult somatic cell reprogrammed to pluripotency by a cocktail of transcription factors (Nobel Prize 2012, Yamanaka).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Multipotent stromal cell (from bone marrow, adipose, etc.) capable of forming bone, cartilage, and fat.
entries.sources: `A. Stem Cells.pptx`, `C. Organs of the Immune System.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Formation and differentiation of blood cells from hematopoietic stem cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Partially specialized cell that can divide and yield differentiated progeny but has limited self-renewal.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cancer of blood-forming cells; may be myelogenous or lymphocytic, acute or chronic.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cancer of lymphocytes; includes Burkitt's, Hodgkin's, non-Hodgkin's types.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Ability of a substance to provoke an adaptive immune response.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Ability of a substance to be specifically recognized/bound by antibody or TCR, regardless of whether it provoked the response.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Gene whose dysregulated activation drives cancer (e.g., c-myc in Burkitt's lymphoma).
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Transcription-factor proto-oncogene activated in Burkitt's lymphoma by translocation to the Ig heavy-chain locus.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Enhanced and faster response to an antigen on re-exposure; basis of vaccination.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Principle that antigen selects pre-existing lymphocyte clones with matching receptors for proliferation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Host cell surface receptor for SARS-CoV-2 spike protein binding; blocked by therapeutic monoclonal antibodies.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: 1273 aa envelope glycoprotein that binds ACE2; encoded by mRNA COVID-19 vaccines.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Sourcing of antibodies or B cells from recovered patients for passive immunity or monoclonal development.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Poxvirus used as smallpox vaccine due to cross-immunity with variola.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cytokine from Hassall's corpuscles that directs thymic dendritic cell maturation.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cell-cell signaling pathway required for T-cell fate specification in the thymus.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Organized contact between T cell and APC integrating TCR, coreceptor and adhesion signaling.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Transmembrane form of antibody that serves as the B-cell receptor; generated by alternative splicing.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Differential splicing that generates secreted or membrane-bound IgM/IgD from the same transcript.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Region of lymph node medulla packed with antibody-secreting plasma cells.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: T-cell-rich region of a lymph node between cortex and medulla.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Outer B-cell-rich region of a lymph node containing follicles.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Host Golgi protease that cleaves HIV gp160 into gp120 and gp41.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Patient T cells engineered to express a CAR targeting tumor antigens; used in cancer immunotherapy.

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Cancer immunotherapy that blocks inhibitory receptors (e.g., CTLA-4, PD-1) to release T-cell antitumor activity (Allison, Nobel).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: Sequence of molecular events from receptor ligation to intracellular response (transducers to transcription factors).

entries.subject: Biology — Immunology
entries.definition: cis-acting regulatory element activated upon V(D)J recombination that drives high Ig transcription in B cells.
entries.sources: `H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx`
